rules: Diana Workbook coordinates win when present and valid.
ArcGIS is only a fallback for unique normalized name + city matches with no address-number contradiction.
US Census Geocoder is optional, free, and treated as medium-confidence street interpolation.
Rows without trusted coordinates remain Needs Location Review and are not ordinary public markers.
The Arts Hub Coordinate Bridge is not read by this script.
summary.censusTermsNote: Official Census docs describe a public geocoder/API for single or batch address geocoding. Returned latitude/longitude values are MAF/TIGER address-range interpolations, so accepted Census rows are medium confidence rather than building certainty.

decisions.name: 112 W. Main St.
decisions.description: This building is known as 'The Brick Store of Silvester and Salamanâ€°' it was built in 1854 and completed in 1855 by Henry Silvester and Abraham Salaman. It is both the first and oldest original brick (and stone) building in Grass Valley and the second oldest brick building in Nevada County. There have only been three owners of this building, Henry Sylvester, Paul Wesley Michell, and Elton and Helen Williams. Only six businesses have occupied the store in 145 years, H. Silvester Grocery, H. J. Fuchs Grocery, C. W. Jenkin Variety Store, Tresize Brothers Stationery Store, Williams Brothers, later changed to Williams Stationery Store, and later became Make Local Habit.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: 400 Broad Street
decisions.description: Text of plaque: It was at this location on the afternoon of June 5, 1880 that a fire erupted in John White's upholstery shop. More than fifty buildings - including the nearby New York Hotel and most of the Chinese Quarter - were destroyed within an hour, but from the ashes of that conflagration a new business district emerged and York Street was created to ensure better downtown fire protection. Then, early in the 20th century, a doctor's office was established here. The longest-serving physician to occupy this corner was Bernard W. Hummelt, MD (1906 - 1998), who, between 1934 and his retirement in 1988, practiced internal medicine, orthopedics and general surgery. Over the course of his lengthy career he delivered over 1500 Nevada County babies. Dr. Hummelt was among the earliest physicians in the nation to administer penicillin and was instrumental in the founding of Sierra Nevada Memorial Hospital - where he served as its third Chief of Staff. Placed by the family of Bernard W. Hummelt, M.D. to honor the centennial of Dr. Hummelt's birth August 5,1906 A remarkable life- an exemplary career.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: 545 Main Street
decisions.description: Druggist and County Treasurer Edward F. Spence built this house on 3 lots in 1866 and lived here with his wife Marietta until her death in 1868. He founded Spenceville, organized banks in San Jose and Southern California, served as Mayor of Los Angeles, and was instrumental in establishing the Mt. Wilson Observatory. County Judge Addison C. Niles bought the house from Spence in 1870 and occupied it with his wife Elizabeth until his election to the California Supreme Court in 1871. The City of Niles, California, is named for him. Niles Searls bought the house from his brother-in-law, Addison Niles, in 1871. When he became a Supreme Court Commissioner in 1885 he and his wife Mary gave the house to son Fred and his bride, Helen Pond, as a wedding present. In 1887 Niles Searls became Chief Justice of the California Supreme Court, and served on the Supreme Court Commission from 1893 to 1899. Attorney Fred Searls (a native of Nevada City) and his wife Helen (a native of Downieville) lived here from 1885 until his death in 1929. The last member of the family to reside here was Fred Searls III, great-grandson of Niles Searls.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Aaron Augustus Sargent Homestead
decisions.description: Homesteads of Nevada Countyâ€™s Statesman, a 49er. Arrived here In 1850 as a journeyman printer. Editor and owner of the countyÃ

decisions.name: Allen Chapman House
decisions.description: Allen Chapman House Built in 1856. The home and dental office of Allen Chapman, Nellie Chapman, and their son C. W. Chapman. Nellie Pooler Chapman was the first woman licensed to practice dentistry in the western United States.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Alpha and Omega Hydraulic
decisions.address: Hwy 20, About 16 m. east of Nevada City and 10 m. west of I-80 Hwy junction. Near eastern end of restrooms in Omega Rest Area
decisions.description: One mile north of here were the towns of Alpha and Omega, named by gold miners in the early 1850â€™s. The tremendous hydraulic diggings, visible from near this point, engulfed most of the original townsites. Alpha was the birthplace of famed opera singer Emma Nevada. Mining at Omega continued until 1949, and lumbering operations are carried on there today.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Alvin Stevens Trivelpiece
decisions.address: At Diggins Overlook in Chute Hill campground about 7/10 m. east of North Bloomfield
decisions.description: Alvin Stevens Trivelpiece 1903-1967. Californian, Clamper, Nevada County Historian, Newspaperman, Friend and a Founding Father of the Malakoff State Park, in whose memory these benches were placed.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: American Hill Diggings
decisions.description: On this site in 1853 Edward E. Matteson developed one of the first hydraulic mines, an operation in which gold was washed from great quantities of earth with high pressure streams of water. The method used here became a standard of the mining industry through out the world.Second plaque dedicated 2006 by Native Sons of the Golden West: The Hydraulic Monitor, Pipe & Gate Valve displayed here was installed by Native Sons of the Golden West, Hydraulic Parlor 56, Nevada City, January 2006. Note: The first approach to hydraulic mining may have been that of Antoine Chabot, said to have used water to direct gravel into the sluicebox at Buckeye Hill in 1852. But Matteson fully developed the system in 1853 by attaching a nozzle to a rawhide hose to wash down soil for gold recovery at American Hill. By the 1860s Nevada County miners universally credited Matteson with the invention of hydraulic mining and other labor-saving devices.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Anthony House Site
decisions.address: East side of Pleasant Valley Rd next to dam spillway at Lake Wildwood
decisions.description: Anthony House and Ranch. In 1849, a Mr. Anthony staked out 160 acres on the north side of Deer Creek on the road which would become part of the Marysville-Henness Pass-Virginia City trade route, and built a house and out-buildings. While further details about Mr. Anthony are unknown, the name Anthony House prevailed through a succession of owners. The property was up for sale in 1852 and purchased by Mr. S. P. French. By that time it was a flourishing stage stop, serving as a hotel, restaurant, livery stable and post office. The original house burned down in 1876 but was quickly rebuilt. Subsequent owners included William Henry Davey, Nevada County Supervisor 1908-1916 and later his son, Frank Davey. Stage stop and post office operations continued to 1906 when the post office was moved to Smartsville. Thereafter, declining traffic on the trade route led to farming and cattle raising as the main use of the property. By 1970 when Boise Cascade began development of this area, the property had fallen into disrepair and the house was burned down as a training exercise by the Penn Valley Fire Protection District. Construction of Anthony Dam here on Deer Creek submerged any remains of this historic site under Lake Wildwood.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Baruh House
decisions.description: The Baruh Home Built 1852. This site and home of two rooms was bought in 1866 by Mr. and Mrs. A. Baruh, grandparents of Mrs. Lucille Sherwood. Mrs. Clara Baruh Quigley, a charter member of Laurel Parlor No. 6, Native Daughters of the Golden West. Mr. Joseph Baruh, son, charter member of Hydraulic Parlor No. 56, Native Sons of the Golden West. Other family members: daughters Jenny Baruh Zellerbach, Rebecca Baruh Gabriel, sons Moses, Marcus and David. Mr. Aaron Baruh was a businessman operating a grocery store on Commercial Street, receiving gold dust for the commodities in the gold rush area. Later he operated a saloon and was very active in the early settlement of Nevada City. Note: Originally built by Jacob Coleman as a two-room cottage, it was expanded in 1866 by Aaron Baruh.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Bell Hill School Bell
decisions.address: Bell Hill School, 342 S. School St
decisions.description: The Bell Hill School Bell 1877. This school bell is lovingly dedicated to the children and teachers of the Grass Valley School District who daily answered its melodious tone. The bell was cast at the Buckeye Bell Foundry in Cincinnati by Vanduzen and Tift, and the rotary yoke was patented in 1868. The bell was placed in this beautiful monument by Harold J. Perry, a member of the Grass Valley Host Lions Club. Dedicated March 30,1985 as a part of the 100th Anniversary Celebration of Quartz Parlor No. 58.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Ben Taylor Home
decisions.description: Benjamin Franklin Taylor (1826-1913) arrived in Grass Valley on September 22, 1849, as part of a group of Mexican War veterans. He then established the Buena Vista Ranch, near the intersection of modern You Bet Road and Highway 174, where he engaged in farming and ranching. Prior to 1863, he bought a large piece of property in Grass Valley, on which in 1865-6 he built the home at what is now 652 Linden Avenue. Mr. Taylor was active in local politics and was part of the partnership that built a toll road between Grass Valley and Colfax, roughly following what is now Dog Bar Road. In 1868, he bought out some of his partners and became the principal owner. As of the time of its landmark registration, the home was still owned by Taylor's descendants.
decisions.reviewNotes: US Census Geocoder result accepted as a free, interpolated street-address coordinate; not storefront/building certainty.

decisions.name: Binkelmann Brewery
decisions.description: Born in Wurtemberg, Germany on January 2, 1828, David Binkelmann came to Grass Valley in 1853 after only a year as a new immigrant in New York. He mined for a time on Wolf Creek, then worked in a bakery. He became a renter of the business, but after two years sold the bakery operation to Herrman Maurer and bought a half-interest in a brewery with John Frank, operating in the rear of the Western Hotel which burned in 1856. The brick brewery was constructed in 1861, replacing a smaller 1856 frame building on North Auburn Street. Frank Richard bought out the partner in 1861 and was in turn bought out by Binkelmann shortly later. He ran the brewery until his death on May 23, 1894, leaving his wife Sophie to carry on until her death in 1915, when the brewery was bequeathed to her son, David J. Binkelmann. Note: The Brewery building, which has a beautiful vaulted brick cellar, has been incorporated into the hotel across the street.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Blue Tent
decisions.address: About 5 miles northeast of Nevada City, approximately at the intersection of N. Bloomfield and Blue Tent School Rds
decisions.description: Blue Tent is a historic mining community established in 1850 and named after its first dwelling, a tent made of blue denim. In its heyday, it had a population of over 350, and its own store, dancehall, blacksmith shop, boarding house and other buildings. A non-denominational Union Church was erected in 1861, and a school was established in 1868. A substantial Chinatown existed just north of Blue Tent. The community was connected by stage to Nevada City, and by the world's first long distance telephone line established in 1878, to the mining communities around the San Juan Ridge. A post office existed between 1878 and 1889. In addition to mining, Blue Tent for many years had a thriving industry harvesting ice and shipping it throughout California. Like many communities that depended on hydraulic mining, Blue Tent began to decline following the 1884 Sawyer decision, forbidding the discharge of mining debris into the Yuba River. As mining declined, agriculture began to predominate. Today, the Blue Tent district is a pleasant, rural, largely residential area. Not much evidence remains of the mining district, except the Blue Tent school, which is still at the site to which it was moved in 1892. It has been converted to a private residence, but remains painted a vivid blue.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Boardman House
decisions.description: Notable as one of the earliest recorded houses in Nevada City and for its history of prominent owners. The tradition that the legendary William Morris Stewart built the house in 1853 cannot be documented for no deeds remain earlier than 1858. However, the house appears as the residence of P. G. Womack in the first map of the city prepared in 1855 and published in 1856. Other owners included the famous mining engineer, Capt. Thos. Mein, whose son William Wallace Mein was born in the house in 1873. The family of the late U.S. Representative Harry Englebright purchased the house in 1897 and resided there until 1959. The interior shows the work of many occupants and the exterior reflects eclectic borrowings from many past styles.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Boca Brewery
decisions.address: East end of Boca Dam, 1 m. north of 1-80 on Stampede Rd, about 5 m. east of Truckee
decisions.description: Boca. Noted as a railroad, ice and lumbering center since 1867 but most fondly remembered and hereby honored as home of the Boca Brewery which was located 1/2 mile south of this point across the Truckee River. Boca Lager Beer was world famous and mercifully slaked the thirst of a grateful mankind. Second plaque placed 2001 by Chap 3691 E Clampus Vitus: Boca Beer. Warranted to keep in any weather.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Boca Dam - Newlands Reclamation Project
decisions.address: East end of Boca Dam, Stampede Road 1 m. north of I-80, about 5 m. east of Truckee
decisions.description: Designated a National Historic Landmark 1981: Boca Dam 1937-1940. United States Department of the Interior, Harold L. Ickes, Secretary; Bureau of Reclamation, John C. Page, Commissioner; R. F. Walter, Chief Engineer; L. J. Foster and C. S. Hale, Construction Engineers. Height 116 feet, Length 1629 feet, Storage capacity 40,901 acre-feet. Contractor, George W. Condon Co. Note: Originally called the Truckee-Carson Project, the Newlands Project covers a large area in western Nevada and a smaller area of Truckee-Tahoe area. One of five authorized by the Federal Reclamation Act of 1902, to reclaim arid lands for agricultural purposes. Renamed in 1919 in honor of U.S. Senator Francis G. Newlands of Nevada, water from natural flow and storage in Lake Tahoe and Boca Reservoir is diverted from Truckee River into the 32.5-mile Truckee Canal and then discharged into the Carson River.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Boca Ice Harvest Site
decisions.address: Stampede Rd, 2 m. north of 1-80, 5 m. east of Truckee
decisions.description: From 1868 to 1927 huge quantities of ice were harvested from the Truckee Basin. This provided employment and significantly contributed to the areaâ€™s economic growth and its colorful history. Boca Pond was one of the first to be used to harvest ice and ship it across the nation. It was one of the last of the ice plants to survive competition from artificial ice companies.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Bridgeport Covered Bridge
decisions.address: Pleasant Valley Rd at South Fork of Yuba River
decisions.description: This covered bridge across the South Fork of the Yuba River at Bridgeport, Nevada County. Built in 1862 by David Isaac John Wood with lumber from his mill in Sierra County, this bridge was part of the Virginia Turnpike Company Toll Road which served the Northern Mines and the busy Nevada Comstock Lode. Utilizing a combination truss and arch construction, it is one of the oldest housed spans in the West and the longest single span, wooden covered bridge in the United States.The plaque is dedicated to those pioneer Americans who came to California in search of a new life and passed this way to establish their homes in the Northern Mine country. Note: The second plaque is full of errors. Bridgeport originally was known as "Nye's Landing" because Urias and Manuel Nye built a trading post here in 1849, but "Nye's Crossing" was on the Middle Fork of the Yuba River, near the mouth of Oregon Creek. Furthermore, the builder's name was David Ingerfield John Wood.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Brimskill Building
decisions.description: Built by Edward Brimskill, 1849-1851. Rear section built 1849. Iron shutters a protection from fire and robbers, imported from Scotland. Used in early days as general store and miners supply. Wells Fargo maintained desk room here for many years and shipped thousands of dollars of gold from here. One of the best preserved buildings in California of the gold rush era. Said to be oldest building in Nevada County.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Calanan Park
decisions.address: Corner Broad and Union Sts, Nevada City. This small park is dedicated to George Calanan and contains several historical objects and plaques.
decisions.description: Text of plaque: Native Daughters Garden in George Calanan Park. In 1922, Laurel Parlor No. 6, Native Daughters of the Golden West acquired two small lots to create a memorial park for the veterans and those who died in World War I. In the interest of 'a valuable public improvement,' Laurel Parlor sold the lot to the U.S. Forest Service for a district office in 1936. The advent of World War II redirected federal resources and the planned 6,000 square foot building was never constructed. The lot remained a community park. In 1967, the park was dedicated to honor George Humphrey Calanan (1872- 1951), a much-loved Town Trustee (City Councilman), health officer, assessor, volunteer fireman, and long time City Clerk. He was known as 'the man who got things done.' The park was granted to the City of Nevada City by the United States Department of the Interior on December 1, 1971.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Calanan Park Drill Core
decisions.description: Shaft Drill Core 1935-1936. Experimental vertical shaft, 5' in diameter to 1125' depth thru serpentine and gabbro at Idaho-Maryland Gold Mine, Grass Valley. Machinery invented by J. B. Newsom. Greatest drilled footage, 21' in three days. Core weight 11-tons. Donated to Nevada City by Kenneth E. Baker.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Calanan Park Monitor
decisions.description: The first hydraulic mining originated on American Hill near Nevada City, California in 1853. This minerâ€™s giant known as the monitor washed hillsides away under high pressure uncovering gold bearing gravel beds. This mining technique flourished profitably until 1884 when the Sawyer decision closed hydraulic mining. Rivers were being filled with mud and debris causing flooding of the rich farmlands below.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Canyon Creek Bridge Site
decisions.address: Maybert Rd at Canyon Creek, 3.3 m. east of town of Washington. Maybert Rd does not require four-wheel-drive but is not advised for cars with low clearance
decisions.description: The Canyon Creek Bridge was built in 1895 by Cotton Brothers and Co. of Oakland, California, for $1,725. Completed two years earlier than the Old Auburn Bridge (No. 13,), it is of similar construction and design. Underneath it is the reservoir that supplies water to the residents of the town of Washington. Canyon Creek is a major tributary of the South Yuba River, and the scenic view of the rapids and falls above the bridge is outstanding. Note: After determining the bridge had outlived its useful life, the County erected a modern bridge in 2017 and placed this plaque: Site of a steel pony truss bridge erected in 1895 at Canyon Creek Crossing on the road linking Washington to the town of Maybert. There the Yuba Mine employed 13 Chinese and 27 Americans, one of the few mines still employing Chinese. In the 1880â€™s and 1890â€™s Maybert had a Post Office, store, boarding house, school and saloon. Fire destroyed all 20 of its dwellings in 1909. The old bridge was disassembled and is awaiting relocation by Nevada City as part of its hiking trails expansion.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Caroline Mead Hanson House
decisions.address: 319 Neal St, corner of S. Church St
decisions.description: Homesite of Caroline Mead Hanson. Whose idea sparked the annual Donation Day sponsored by Grass Valley Ladies Relief Society since 1883. Note: Home of the originator of Grass Valley's Donation Day, that began In 1883. Mrs. Hansen, an invalid, sat in the bay window of her home and watched the children troop to school. Hearing of the Ladies Relief SocietyÃ
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Charles Marsh House
decisions.description: Built about 1866 by Charles Marsh, who arrived here in July, 1849, in what would later become Nevada City. Marsh was the first County Surveyor and the first County Supervisor from Nevada City. He created the water distribution system which formed the basis for P.G.& E. and the Nevada Irrigation District. Marsh was influential in the routing of the Central Pacific Railroad and served on its original Board of Directors, as well as the Board of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. He was also instrumental in creating a Library Association in Nevada City.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Cherokee
decisions.address: North of Nevada City on Tyler Foote Rd, about 5 miles east of Hwy 49, just past Sages Rd
decisions.description: Cherokee is a former gold mining community In Nevada County, located on the San Juan Ridge about 4 miles east of North San Juan. At the time of the Gold Rush, this area was occupied by the Nisenan. The Gold Rush came to Cherokee in 1850, when a group of Cherokee Indians began mining there and gave it its name. The town's heyday was between 1856 and 1865. It had two hotels, the Grizzly and Curnowâ€™s, saloons, several stores, a butcher, a shoe shop, a resident physician and drugstore, and a blacksmith. Its sawmill had a capacity of 10,000 ft. of lumber a day. The local newspaper was the Cherokee Journal. There was stagecoach service to Nevada City. After 1878, Cherokee was connected to the worldâ€™s first long distance telephone line which connected the mining communities along the San Juan Ridge from French Corral to the Bowman Lake area. The town began to decline in the 1880â€™s, after hydraulic mining was restricted by the Sawyer decision. By 1924, the town was reduced to "one store; and the people live principally by farming, very little active mining being carried on here." Today, all that remains of Cherokee is the schoolhouse located on Sages Road just north of Tyler Foote Crossing Road, the historic cemetery, and a lot of land scarred by hydraulic mining.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Chicago Park Historic Agricultural Community
decisions.address: Along Hwy 174 between the Bear River and Brunswick Rd
decisions.description: The Chicago Park Historic Agricultural Community occupies roughly the area on either side of Highway 174 between the Bear River and Brunswick Road. It is centered on the villages of Chicago Park and Peardale. Prior to the 1840s, this area was populated by the Nisenan. In August 1849, what is reported to have been Nevada County's second store was erected on land overlooking the junction of the Bear River and Greenhorn Creek. In the 1850s, local residents led by Simmon Storms succeeded in having the Nisenan removed to a reservation near Tehama. During that period, Benjamin Taylor settled on a ranch in the area which he named Buena Vista, after the Mexican War battle in which he had participated, and engaged in farming and horse breeding. Others who settled in the area found it very suitable for growing fruit, especially pears. In 1872, the Coleman Brothers purchased the Buena Vista and Storms ranches, and other properties in the area, as they were developing the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. Completed in 1876, the Railroad spurred the agricultural development of the area. In 1887, a group of Colfax and Chicago, Illinois developers acquired several thousand acres of land and drew up plans for the Chicago Park Colony. The Colony was to consist of 20 and 40 acre tracts as well as town lots laid out around a public square with streets named for prominent streets in Chicago. Much of the planned development never materialized, but reportedly almost 7000 acres were sold, principally to residents of Chicago. Many of the tracts were planted with orchards, and the area was referred to as the Bartlett Pear Belt. The railroad station at Storms Station was renamed Chicago Park Station, There were also stations at Buena Vista and Peardale. Fruit was king in the area for about 100-years. Orchards growing pears, plums, peaches and apples were everywhere. Because water was not abundant, dry farming techniques were mastered which improved fruit color. In 1915, local Bartlett pears were honored at the Pacific International Exposition in San Francisco. As the 20th century progressed, pear blight appeared, causing some growers to switch to other crops.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Chief Truckee
decisions.address: Truckee River Regional Park, Hwy. 267, 1 m. south of Truckee
decisions.description: Chief Truckee. The legendary Paiute leader; friend and guide to pioneers; breveted a 'Captain' by John C. Fremont; gave his name to this valley, river and town; died near Dayton, Nevada, in 1860.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: China Wall
decisions.address: 14365 Old Rough and Ready Hwy, Rough and Ready
decisions.description: Third plaque: The China Wall. In the 1850s, Chinese laborers, who were an essential part of the community, built the wall you see before you. Large natural stones were hand stacked for its construction. The wall extends 5 miles in either direction, however much of it has disintegrated over the years. This lot, along with the lot across the Gephardt's Union Turnpike, and the adjoining lots, are referred to in the Nevada County tax records as the Ã¯China LotsÃ

decisions.name: China Wall at Donner Summit
decisions.address: South side of Donner Pass Rd (Old Hwy 40), west of Donner Lake about 0.5 m. below summit
decisions.description: Charles Crocker, construction chief of the Central Pacific Railroad (CPRR), contracted for a workforce of approximately 12,000 Chinese laborers to push the CPRR tracks over its Trans-Sierra crossing on its race east to meet with the Union Pacific at Promontory, Utah Territory. A railroad retaining wall and fill, constructed of Sierra granite, stand silently above on the pass as a lasting monument to these Asian 'Master Builders' who left an indelible mark on the history of California and the West.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Chinatown Grass Valley (Site)
decisions.address: Corner of Tinloy and Bank Sts
decisions.description: Chinatown, Grass Valley 1850-1938. Of the many gold rush Immigrants the Chinese were noted for their honest, sober and industrious characteristics. Each mining camp had its Chinatown and Grass Valley was second only to San FranciscoÃ
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Chinese Cemetary
decisions.description: The 1852 state census recorded 3,396 Chinese living in Nevada County, about 20% of its population. Soon, anti-Chinese sentiment forced the Chinese out of the cemeteries managed by white people and into their own segregated cemetery. By the 1870s they began burial at the site of the present Chinese Cemetery. The inscription on the shrine inside the Cemetery states that the Cemetery was "reconstructed" on August 6, 1891, a date that also appears on the original gate at the entrance of the Cemetery. The site is owned presently by the Thiem family who operate the Inn Town Campground which surrounds the Cemetery, with the Chinese Historical Society having a historic preservation easement.
decisions.reviewNotes: US Census Geocoder result accepted as a free, interpolated street-address coordinate; not storefront/building certainty.

decisions.name: Chris Stevens Court
decisions.description: tevens Court honors John Christopher Stevens, United States Ambassador to Libya, who died in the service of his country September 11, 2012, in Benghazi. 'Chris' Stevens was born in Nevada County, the offspring of three generations of Grass Valleyans. Chris loved Grass Valley and spent many happy hours there. His fascination in foreign affairs and history led him to a Peace Corps stint in Morocco, followed by work in international legal law practice in San Francisco and Washington D.C. He then joined the Foreign Service, in which he served in Egypt, Saudi Arabia, Tunisia, Jerusalem and Syria. When revolution broke out in Libya, Chris was sent out on a rusty Greek freighter to report and make recommendations for U.S. policy. His work led to appointment as U.S. Ambassador to the fledgling nation. Early on he established rapport with the Libyans, as he had with the people of the Middle East and Africa wherever he was stationed with the Foreign Service. Rather than hiding behind fortified walls, he strolled the streets, talking to the people. One day after the attack that cost him his life, thousands of Libyans took to the streets protesting his death. They carried hand printed signs, some reading: 'Chris Stevens was a friend to all Libyans'.
decisions.reviewNotes: No Diana coordinate and no confident ArcGIS fallback. Address-bearing rows are eligible for the optional free Census Geocoder pass.

decisions.name: Chuck Yeager Monument
decisions.address: Nevada County Airport, John Bauer Ave.
decisions.description: A series of 5 plaques chronicles the career of Gen. Charles E. "Chuck" Yeager, a distinguished aviator, war hero and test pilot, who retired to this area. An F-104 Starfighter, the world's first Mach 2 operational aircraft which Yeager tested in 1954, is strikingly displayed behind the plaques.

decisions.name: Clinton Narrow Gauge Railroad
decisions.address: Southeast corner of Hirschdale Rd, and Juniper Way, about 5 m. east of Truckee and 1 m. south of I-80
decisions.description: First steam railroad logging operation in the Truckee River Basin. Commenced operation in the late 1870â€™s under the supervision of Fred Burckhalter, head of the Pacific Lumber and Wood Co. Line extended 10 miles along Juniper Creek with hope of eventual service to Lake Tahoe. Line abandoned in 1901 after several conflagrations at the PL&W Co. mill in Clinton.

decisions.name: Colley House
decisions.description: Queen Anne Style Built in 1860 Home of Ida Colley and niece, Winifred Mulloy, founders of Laurel Parlor #6 Native Daughters of the Golden West. Now Deer Creek Inn Bed & Breakfast.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Columbia Hill School
decisions.address: Tyler Foote Rd, San Juan Ridge, 8.7 m. east of Hwy 49, northeast of Nevada City
decisions.description: The schoolhouse, built in 1875, was still in use as a school in the 1970's. In 1980 the State of California condemned the schoolhouse as unsafe. It was remodeled and used for a while as a community center. Later a new community center was established next door, and the old school building was used for other purposes. It is now known as the North Columbia Schoolhouse Cultural Center. The original Columbia Hill School was built prior to March 11,1867.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Commercial Row / Brickelltown Historic District
decisions.description: The Commercial Row/ Brickelltown Historic District lies in the center of Truckee along the tracks of the first transcontinental railroad. It includes the first built areas of Truckee. Because of fires, most of the buildings in the district range from 1870 to the 1920s. The district illustrates Truckee's relationship with the railroad and the lumber industry and Truckee's appearance more than 100-years ago. Truckee was originally called Coburn Station, and then renamed around 1868 after a Paiute chief. The District was dedicated in 2009 by the Truckee Town Council.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Crabtree House
decisions.description: Lotta Crabtree was born in New York in 1847. In 1852 the gold fever brought Mr. Crabtree to California, and a year later his wife and Lotta followed. Several months after arriving in San Francisco Mrs. Crabtree and Lotta went to Grass Valley and started a boarding house for miners with Mr. Crabtree. It was here that Lotta met Lola Montez, who taught her to sing and dance. Lotta made her first public appearance in Plumas County, which led to a successful career on stage, both here and abroad.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Davis Mill
decisions.address: Off North Bloomfield Rd; 3 m. NE of Nevada City. Not open to the public
decisions.description: The Davis Mill is a well preserved stamp mill which served a small gold mine run by the Davis family from 1915 to 1940.
decisions.reviewNotes: No Diana coordinate and no confident ArcGIS fallback. Address-bearing rows are eligible for the optional free Census Geocoder pass.

decisions.name: Donner Camp at Alder Creek Valley
decisions.address: Alder Creek Picnic Site; 2.75 m. north of 1-80 on Hwy 89, 0.1 m. east of Hwy 89, Truckee
decisions.description: This site has three plaques and was designated a National Historical Landmark in 1966. First plaque dedicated 1996 by Tahoe National Forest: In the fall of 1846, 25 members of the Donner Party became trapped by an early snowstorm here at Alder Creek Valley. The George and Jacob Donner families, their teamsters, and fellow travelers suffered extreme hardship and starvation. They spent the winter here cut off from the rest of their party who camped at Donner Lake. Only eleven survived the ordeal. Their survival, against desperate odds, stands as a testament to the enduring pioneer spirit that helped shape the future of California. Second plaque dedicated 1996 by Chap 3691 E Clampus Vitus: They gave unselfishly, their fortunes and their lives that their children should survive. Near this site, in the winter of 1846, two pioneer women gave up their lives for their families. Tamsen and Elizabeth Donner feared their many children could not survive the ravages of cold and starvation when the party was caught in an early blizzard. They provided care and comfort to their families and companions throughout the snowbound winter, desperately trying to prevent the death of their loved ones. Both lost their lives. However, most of their children survived to carry their mothers' dreams of new life and new beginnings to the valleys of California. The summit that they never crossed now bears the family name. An inspiration to all who followed their footsteps across the Sierra Nevada Mountains, we herein honor the memory and the sacrifices of these women. Their struggle to survive, enduring hardships we can barely imagine, remains a legacy of the pioneer spirit. On this 150th anniversary of their tragic encampment the Chief Truckee Chapter of E Clampus Vitus dedicates this monument to their spirit and the preservation of their memory that we not forget the sacrifices they made in opening California to its destiny. The Donner Camp Site plaque is located in the middle of the Donner Camp Interpretive Trail, just past the end of the boardwalk. Second plaque dedicated 1960 by Chap 10 E Clampus Vitus, Historical Society, Tahoe National Forest USFS, Truckee-Donner Chamber of Commerce, and Native Daughters of the Golden West: On October 28, 1846 the six covered wagons brought west by George and Jacob Donner and their families halted here for repairs. By March of 1847 one half of the party of 22 adults and children had died of starvation and cold. They came west seeking a new life and found misery and death.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Donner Emigrant Trail
decisions.address: Front of Tahoe Truckee High School, 11725 Donner Pass Rd, Truckee
decisions.description: The Emigrant Trail in the Pioneer Days of California came through the low pass to the north, facing this monument. The Trail turned west at this point for a distance of twenty-six hundred feet where a tablet describes the route then followed. Adjoining plaque dedicated 2014: First Permanent Emigrant Trail Markers. The nearby monument was envisioned in 1929 by C.F. McGlashan, Truckeeâ€™s foremost resident 1872-1931 and author of the 1880 classic, The History of the Donner Party; P.M. Weddell, who placed wooden signs on the Donner Trail from Verdi, Nevada over Coldstream Pass to Lake Mary from 1924 until his death in 1951; and W.F. Knowland, U. S. Senator (1945-1959) and the chairman of The Native Sons of the Golden Westâ€™s Landmark Commission. The three trail authorities met in McGlashanâ€™s law office in Truckee to draw up the text for the plaque on this monument, listed as #3 on the 1930 Weddell map to the right and a companion plaque for the monument listed as #5 located one half mile to the west on old Hwy 40 and Coldstream Road. Note: According to the P. M. Weddell map of the Historic Emigrant Road, this resting place of the Native Sons' marker is close to its original location.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Donner Memorial State Park
decisions.address: 12593 Donner Pass Rd, Truckee. In addition to the Museum, the Park contains three monuments.
decisions.description: The Origin Of The Park. Joseph Marzen sold one acre of land east of Donner Lake to Charles F. McGlashan for the purpose of erecting a monument to the Donner Party. McGlashan then sold the land to the Native Sons of the Golden West (NSGW) for $1.00 in August of 1909. At the center of the acre, according to McGlashan, was the site of the Breen Cabin, where the monument would be situated. On June 6, 1918, the NSGW dedicated the completed Pioneer Memorial. California Governor William D. Stephens signed legislation acquiring 'Donner Monument and lands contingent thereto.' On May 23, 1928, the Grand Parlor of the Native Sons of the Golden West conveyed eleven acres of land to the People of the State of California. The deed for the parcel adjacent to the Lincoln Highway included one provision: 'The tract of land above described shall be known and designated as the Donner State Monument and shall be a memorial to the California Pioneers of the Overland Trailâ€™. Note: The plaque is inside the building, to the left as you enter.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Donner or Pioneer Monument
decisions.address: In Donner Memorial State Park, about 100 yds. east of Museum
decisions.description: Monument dedicated and first two plaques placed 1918 by the Governors of California and Nevada, three survivors of the Donner Party, and Native Daughters of the Golden West. First plaque: Virile to risk and find, Kindly withal and a ready help. Facing the brunt of fate. Indomitable-unafraid." Second plaque: In commemoration of the pioneers who crossed the plains to settle in California. Landmark registered and third plaque placed 1962 by State of California, CHL 134: Donner Party. Near this spot stood the Breen Cabin of the party of emigrants who started for California from Springfield, Illinois, in April, 1846, under the leadership of Captain George Donner. Delays occurred and when the party reached this locality, on October 29, the Truckee Pass emigrant road was concealed by snow. The height of the shaft of the monument indicates the depth of the snow, which was twenty-two feet. After futile efforts to cross the summit the party was compelled to encamp for the winter. The Graves cabin was situated about three- quarters of a mile to the eastward, the Murphy cabin was about two hundred yards southwest of the monument, and the Donner tents were at the head of Alder Creek. Ninety people were in the party and forty-two perished, most of them from starvation and exposure.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Donner Party - Graves Cabin Site
decisions.address: Opposite 12020 Donner Pass Rd in West Truckee
decisions.description: Plaque dedicated 1919 by Native Sons of the Golden West: Donner Party 1846-1847. On this spot stood the Graves Cabin.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Donner Summit Bridge
decisions.address: Donner Pass Rd (Old Hwy 40) overlooking Donner Lake
decisions.description: First plaque dedicated 1926 by Native Sons of the Golden West: Dedicated to the pioneers who blazed the Overland Trail through these mountains. Built by the U. S. Bureau of Public Roads and the California Highway Commission, 1925-1926. Landmark registered and second plaque dedicated 1998 by Nevada County: 1926 Donner Summit Bridge Restored 1995. Note: In 1926 it was anticipated that the bridge (built at a cost of about $40,000) would become a popular stopping place on the "Victory Highway." The bridge was designed by Norman C. Raab, whose wife, Ruby Bishop Raab, was the great-granddaughter of Isaac Hitchcock of the Stephens-Townsend-Murphy party. The original bridge arch was 110 feet long and 70 feet high, built on a compound curve, with a seven percent grade. Among those who attended the 1926 ceremony were Charles F. McGlashan and these descendants of Donner party survivors: James Fraser Lewis, Martha Jane Lewis, Susan A. Lewis, and Ernest H. Murphy. Norman Raab also designed the world-famous Bixby Creek Bridge on State Highway 1 at Big Sur, built in 1930-1931.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Donner Summit Tunnel
decisions.address: On Donner Pass Rd, about 3.8 mi. south of I-80, Truckee. Turn at Donner Summit Elevation 7,135 ft. marker. In the Lamson-Cash Donner Summit Hub across parking lot from Summit Haus.
decisions.description: "Great Summit Tunnel of the Sierra Nevada. Beneath this plaque the first Transcontinental Railroad traversed the mighty Sierra Nevada range. The 1659 foot Long summit tunnel took over 15 months of Chinese muscle and sweat to build. The Chinese painstakingly hand drilled, then blasted the granite rock with black powder and newly invented nitroglycerine. A vertical shaft took 85 days to complete and allowed tunnel construction to proceed from the center as well as from the portals. The most difficult obstacle facing the Central Pacific Railroad was overcome when the tunnel was completed in August 1867. The first passenger train passed through the tunnel on June 18, 1868. The last train went through in 1993."
decisions.reviewNotes: No Diana coordinate and no confident ArcGIS fallback. Address-bearing rows are eligible for the optional free Census Geocoder pass.

decisions.name: Doris Foley Library of Historical Research
decisions.description: Nevada City Public Library. Library Association formed December 19, 1857. First library was in Kidd and Knox Building, 228 Broad Street. It was moved to Oustamah Lodge IOOF No. 16 June 3, 1874. Nevada City Trustees assumed responsibilities October 1, 1902 and was moved into the Corcoran Building next to the Nevada Theater. The Carnegie Endowment was given to the city in 1907. The present library was dedicated October 4, 1907. City groups raised funds for furnishings and beautified grounds 1911. Note: In 1996 the Nevada City Council, the Nevada County Board of Supervisors, and the Nevada County Historical Landmarks Commission voted to rename this library for Doris Foley, an outstanding historian and author who contributed greatly to scholarly research in this county. In addition to her writings, she helped found the Nevada County Historical Society and was the first librarian at Searls Historical Library. The Doris Foley Library of Historical Research was formally dedicated on May 17,1997.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Dow Alexander Park
decisions.description: Formerly known as the City Square, this park was established by the Grass Valley Women's Improvement Club in 1911, as a place of rest for patrons of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. It was dedicated to the pioneers and veterans of the Spanish American War. Second plaque: Last on duty at Fort Point on San Francisco Bay. Grass Valley Womenâ€™s Improvement Club arranged with the US War Department for donation to be placed in City Square, now Dow Alexander Park. Transferred from Benicia Arsenal to here in 1911. Only known example of its type to exist. Weight: 9100 pounds.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Dr. W. C. Jones Home Site
decisions.address: 129 S Auburn St, now Grass Valley Police Dept
decisions.description: Dr. W. C. Jones came to Grass Valley in 1874 after serving as physician and surgeon for the Central Pacific Railroad Co. during construction of the railroad between Colfax and Reno. He had three sons, Will, John, and Carl, who along with their father served the community as doctors until the last son died in 1944. In 1904 Dr. John Jones opened the first private hospital in Nevada County-the W. C. Jones Memorial Hospital, Grass Valley. A fourth son, George, became Superior Court Judge of Nevada County, serving from 1908 until 1925 and again from 1938 until he retired in 1947. Note: The city removed the historic house to make room for the police department.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Edward Coleman House
decisions.description: The Edward Coleman House. Edward Coleman built this house in 1866. While residing here, Coleman was President/Superintendent of the Idaho Mine, Director of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad, and had interests in the Mohawk Lumber Company. Coleman sold this house in 1893.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Edwards Crossing Bridge
decisions.address: On North Bloomfield Rd at South Fork of Yuba River, northeast of Nevada City
decisions.description: Built in 1904 by the American Bridge Company of steel-connection-pin design to replace a wooden structure built in 1862 by William Edwards. In 1854 Edwards began operations here with a ferry, and in 1855 he built a bridge and toll road between Blue Tent and Lake City. His first bridge washed away in December 1861, and was replaced in the following year. The county acquired the bridge sometime after 1880. Note: Both this and the similar Gault bridge at Nevada City (No. 99) were built under the supervision of Nevada County Surveyor W. W. Waggoner.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Elk's Lodge
decisions.description: "After constructing a second–story meeting hall above two existing 19th century commercial buildings, the Nevada City Benevolent and Protective Order of Elks #518 dedicated its first lodge at this location on June 14, 1913. The lodge hall and street–level storefronts served the community until a devastating fire broke out on the morning of March 20, 2002. Property owners Ken and Kay Baker and Gary and Patty Tintle then initiated a reconstruction program that has resulted in the current building – faithful to the architecture and style of the historic structure it replaced."
decisions.geocodedAddress: 111 N. Pine St, Nevada City, CA,
decisions.reviewNotes: US Census Geocoder result accepted as a free, interpolated street-address coordinate; not storefront/building certainty.

decisions.name: Ellen Sargent
decisions.description: "Ellen Clark Sargent, a native of Massachusetts, arrived in Nevada City in 1852. A leader in the fight for women's right to vote, she founded the Nevada County Woman's Suffrage Association in 1869. She was the President of the California Woman Suffrage Association and Treasurer of the National Woman Suffrage Association. Her tireless work resulted in California women winning the right to vote in 1911. Her campaign for national women's suffrage led to the introduction in 1878 of the 19th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution by her husband, U.S. Senator A. A. Sargent. After many hard-fought battles by the Women's Suffrage Movement, the 19th Amendment was finally enacted into law on August 26, 1920."
decisions.reviewNotes: No Diana coordinate and no confident ArcGIS fallback. Address-bearing rows are eligible for the optional free Census Geocoder pass.

decisions.name: Emigrant Trail on Coldstream Road
decisions.address: West side of Cold Stream Rd, 800 ft south of Donner Pass Rd, just past Cold Stream Day Use sign, Truckee
decisions.description: The Emigrant Trail in the pioneer days of California turned to the south at this point for approximately three miles, then went across the summit of the Sierras about a mile south of the present railroad. It was here that the Donner Party missed the trail owing to the early snows, resulting in the tragedy.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Emigrant Trail to Nevada City
decisions.address: Washington Ridge Vista Point, Hwy 20, 0.2 m. west of Washington Rd
decisions.description: In 1850, the year after the fabulous gold strike on Deer Creek in Nevada City, the Overland Emigrant Trail branched off the original Bear River route at this point on its way to Nevada City and the Sacramento Valley. Soon thereafter the trail became a toll road and later a public road. The deep vehicle ruts can still be seen as they cross and recross Highway 20 many times.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Emmanuel Episcopal Church
decisions.description: Historic Emmanuel Episcopal Church. Oldest Episcopal edifice in California. First services April 23, 1854 by Rt. Rev. William I. Kip, Bishop of Calif. This building opened for worship Aug. 1856. Note: The church was declared eligible for National Landmark status in 1982, but the vestry declined to have the church registered. The first services mentioned above were held in "a public hall" according to an account by Bishop Kip. On December 11, 1856, the Gold Hill Mining Company donated land to the parish that was bounded by Church, Walsh and Mill streets. The present church opened for worship in 1858. It is the oldest existing Episcopal Church in continuous use in California.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Empire House
decisions.description: This site at the lower end of Boston Ravine on the old main road from Grass Valley to Auburn has been the location of a public house since 1851. Charles Behrisch was the owner from 1851 to 1885. Ownership changed hands many times through the years. Purchased by Dominic Pajnich on August 1,1933, and was burned in 1934. Replaced with a 14-room hotel, named Empire Inn. Upstairs closed off in 1964, but remains a popular food and beverage stop today. Boston Ravine, established in 1849, was the earliest mining community in the area which subsequently grew into Grass Valley.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Empire Mine
decisions.address: Empire Mine State Historic Park, 338 E. Empire St
decisions.description: This plaque marks the site of the Empire Mine, noted for its continuous operation 1850-1957 producing over $120,000,000 in gold.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Englebright House
decisions.description: Notable as one of the earliest recorded houses in Nevada City and for its history of prominent owners. The tradition that the legendary William Morris Stewart built the house in 1853 cannot be documented for no deeds remain earlier than 1858. However, the house appears as the residence of P. G. Womack in the first map of the city prepared in 1855 and published in 1856. Other owners included the famous mining engineer, Capt. Thos. Mein, whose son William Wallace Mein was born in the house in 1873. The family of the late U.S. Representative Harry Englebright purchased the house in 1897 and resided there until 1959. The interior shows the work of many occupants and the exterior reflects eclectic borrowings from many past styles.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: English and New Salmon Mine Stamp Mill
decisions.description: This stamp mill was manufactured at the Miners Foundry and Supply Co., Nevada City, California, in the late 1800â€™s. After a circuitous journey, it has returned to its place of origin. It started its industrial life of crushing hard rock at the English Mine, and then the New Salmon Mine, owned by Donald McPherson, at Badger Hill. The stamp mill ceased operation in 1942 when all non-critical gold mines were shut down. After sitting idle through the years overgrown by trees and brush the stamp mill was moved in the 1970â€™s to the Nevada City home of King McPherson, who generously donated the stamp mill back to the Miners Foundry in the 1980â€™s. The stamp mill sat in pieces around the foundry, despite several attempts to reconstruct it. Through the efforts of the members of Hydraulic Parlor No. 56, Native Sons of the Golden West under the supervision of Mike Nevius, restoration was completed in the spring of 2000.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Ernie Bierwagen
decisions.address: Larsen Rd about 1000 ft from the Orchard Springs access road.
decisions.description: Ernie was born February 1915 and died in February 2004, in Chicago Park, California. He was close to the land and the people he met along the way. He was a timeless leader in the agricultural community, his church, and was a friend to many. Always genuine, he was a man of strong faith that sustained him and those around him. Ernie and his family devoted their lives to making this world a better place. The love of the community by the Bierwagen Family continues to this day. Ernie often brought his family to observe the construction of this beautiful Reservoir. He knew the importance of water to nature, the food supply and the lives of all people. Ernie served 25 years as a Nevada Irrigation District Division II Director. With passion, he strived to protect and enhance the water supply. To all that visit this beautiful area, please enjoy.
decisions.reviewNotes: No Diana coordinate and no confident ArcGIS fallback. Address-bearing rows are eligible for the optional free Census Geocoder pass.

decisions.name: Felix Gillet
decisions.address: Stone pillar by driveway at intersection of Nursery and Nihell Sts, Nevada City
decisions.description: Here lived and worked Felix Gillet, A Frenchman by birth, an American by adoption from 1871 to 1908 through whose vision and perseverance the horticulture of the world was enriched by the propagation and culture of walnuts, filberts, chestnuts, prunes. In commemoration of his work this tablet was set March 7, 1933 by the WomenÃ
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Fippin's Blacksmith Shop
decisions.description: Republic of Rough and Ready April 1850 - July 1850. This community of 3000 people seceded from the Union to declare a free and independent state. Colonel E. F. Brundage was elected president. Congressional action on California statehood was pending at the time. Built in the mid 1850s by John Fippin and John Single. Fippin married Single's daughter Julia in 1867, and they raised 10 children. Local legend has it that one of Lotta Crabtree's earliest performances was atop the anvil here. The building today is a combination of the original blacksmith shop and Single's carpenter shop. W. H. (Bill) Fippin and Frank A. Fippin followed their father as smiths and ran the business until well into the 20th century. Members of the Fippin and Single families have served Rough and Ready for generations as County officials, School Trustees and Postmasters.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: First Brick Building in Nevada City
decisions.description: Built in 1853. First brick building erected in this city. Dedicated to the memory of the pioneers of Nevada City. Note: This originally was the Dickerman carpenter shop. Bricks for this building were manufactured at Zeno P. Davis's brickyard on Lower Grass Valley Rd. in Nevada City (now called Searls Ave).
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: First Commercial Winery/305 Broad St.
decisions.description: "In the 1850s, over 100,000 grapevines were planted in Nevada County, often by French settlers on the western part of the San Juan Ridge. For some years, Augustine Isoard operated a wine and liquor store in this building. On June 1, 1860, he advertised in the Nevada Journal that "I have on hand a few gallons of Wine manufactured from the Grape by myself, in this city." This is believed to be the first commercial winery operation in Nevada County. After a lull in the middle of the 20th century, commercial grape growing and winemaking came roaring back. Nevada County became part of the Sierra Foothills American Viticultural Area in 1987 and home to many wineries and hundreds of acres of grapes."
decisions.reviewNotes: US Census Geocoder result accepted as a free, interpolated street-address coordinate; not storefront/building certainty.

decisions.name: First Long Distance Telephone
decisions.address: In front of private home at 20342 Pleasant Valley Rd, four m. west of Hwy 49
decisions.description: Built in 1877, the Ridge Telephone Co., connected French Corral with French Lake 58 miles away. It was operated by the Milton Mining Co., from this building which was built about 1853. This plaque donated by Sadie Winn Brainard. Note: This plaque was originally placed on the brick building in which the telephone office was located. After the building was razed in 1955 it was placed on a concrete monument on the shoulder of the present Pleasant Valley Road in front of a neighboring residence. The 1877 date is an error. The line began operation in 1878. The claim of being the "world's first" is still being debated. However, its challengers did not have the track record of the Ridge Telephone Company, which operated successfully into the 20th century over a much longer distance.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: First Motorcycle Across Sierra
decisions.address: Inside Museum of Truckee History at Amtrak Station, 10065 Donner Pass Rd., Truckee CA
decisions.description: "George Adams Wyman. The first person to cross America by motorcycle, riding from San Francisco to New York City. May 16–July 6, 1903. George A. Wyman was the first person to make a transcontinental Journey across America by means of a motorized vehicle. Wyman rode a 200cc, 1.25-horsepower 1902 'California' motor-bicycle manufactured by the California Motor Company. On May 20th, Wyman departed the Summit Hotel at Donner Pass. He followed the railroad tracks through the snow sheds to intercept the road near the head of Donner Lake. He continued another 10 miles to Truckee, where he asked for directions and was urged to take the Prosser House road to the Boca siding. At Boca, he switched back to the tracks, passing through Verdi on to Reno, where he stopped for the night."
decisions.reviewNotes: No Diana coordinate and no confident ArcGIS fallback. Address-bearing rows are eligible for the optional free Census Geocoder pass.

decisions.name: First Transcontinental Railroad--Truckee
decisions.address: Track side of Amtrak station, 10065 Donner Pass Rd
decisions.description: While construction on Sierra tunnels delayed Central Pacific, advance forces at Truckee began building forty miles of track east and west of Truckee, moving all supplies by wagon and sled. Summit Tunnel was opened in December, 1867. The line reached Truckee April 3, 1868, and the Sierra was conquered. Rails reached Reno June 19, and construction advanced eastward one mile daily toward the meeting with Union Pacific at Promontory May 10,1869, to complete the first transcontinental railroad.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: First Wagons Across the Sierra
decisions.address: West end Donner Lake, corner Donner Pass Rd and S. Shore Dr, west of Truckee
decisions.description: The Stephens-Townsend-Murphy Party, with guidance from old Caleb Greenwood and Chief Truckee, brought their wagons up the Truckee River bed and on Nov. 25,1844, 191 days out of Council Bluffs, Iowa, passed this point and crossed the mighty Sierra summit directly to the west, blazing the famous California Emigrant Trail for all who followed.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Five-Stamp Mill 1893
decisions.description: Used to crush gold ore at the Fortuna Mine near Nevada City. Ore fed under iron 'stamps' from rear hopper. Steam, water or electric belt-driven power turned wheel which revolved cams. Donated to Nevada City by Bill Magers.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Floriston (Bronco Site)
decisions.address: Eastbound Floriston offramp on I-80 east of Truckee
decisions.description: Established in 1872 as Bronco, this railroad wood stop became a noted ice and lumber center and was renamed in 1891. The Floriston Pulp and Paper Company built the present town after 1899 and operated one of the worldÃ
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Foote's Crossing Road
decisions.address: From intersection of Cruzon Grade and Tyler-Foote Rds in Nevada County to Alleghany in Sierra County (See Note on access below)
decisions.description: Footeâ€™s Crossing Road was built by A. D. Foote in 1913 as an all-season short-cut from Columbia Hill to the mines of the Alleghany area. Foote was president of the Tightner Mine Co., the largest gold producer in the region at that time. The road, a 15-foot shelf hanging on the sheer canyon wall above the Middle Fork of the Yuba River, is a spectacular engineering feat and scenic wonder. The rock walls, built without mortar are still intact today. Unforeseen difficulties in building the road caused it to far exceed specifications so Foote personally financed 60% of the total cost, the balance being paid by Nevada and Sierra counties and the U.S. Forest Service.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Gaston
decisions.address: Roughly midway between Graniteville and town of Washington, near the intersection of Gaston and Hoosier Rds, in the Tahoe National Forest.
decisions.description: A historic mining community. Placer mining around Gaston began in the early 1850â€™s. The first hard rock mine, the California Mine, was established in 1856. An ample supply of water was ensured by the arrival of the North Bloomfield ditch in 1870. In 1898, the mine, now known as the Gaston Mine, was acquired by the San Francisco based Gaston Gold Mining Company, which substantially upgraded operations. A town blossomed around the mine. The population was recorded as about 200 in 1900. There was stage service to the town of Washington, and to Nevada City. By 1904, the town boasted two stores, a hotel, a saloon, a hardware store, a Post Office, a water system, electric lights, a telephone line to Nevada City, a fire company and a school. The mine closed during World War I, because of a shortage of labor and supplies needed for the war effort. There was intermittent mining after the war. By the 1940sâ€™, the last remaining building was in shambles. Today, nothing readily visible remains of Gaston.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Gateway Cabin
decisions.description: This cabin was one of several built by Jack Wolert for Dick Joseph in 1939 on US Route 40. The cabin was constructed of lumber salvaged from houses being dismantled five miles north of Truckee at Hobart Mills and served as the Gateway Motelâ€™s office. Originally located at the site of the current Gateway Shopping Center on Donner Pass Road (historic US 40). It was donated to the Truckee Donner Historical Society in 1990 and moved to this location on May 23, 1991. Thanks to community efforts, the cabin has been restored and serves as a historical research library.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: George Schaffer
decisions.description: One of Truckeeâ€™s earliest settlers, Schaffer in 1866 built the first lumber mill in the town. He freighted the locomotive 'San Mateo' to Truckee in a winter crossing of the Sierra prior to the laying of Central Pacific tracks. This building 'The Star Hotel' was built by Schaffer probably for his family. When mill operations were relocated up Martis Creek in 1871, this building was converted to a hotel and has remained in continuous operation to this day. For 24 years, the Truckee Basin supplied timber to railroads, mines and boom towns through 'Uncle George's' enterprises. Note: The Schaffer family cemetery, located at 10233 Schaffer Dr., may be visited by the public.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Gilmore Flying Field
decisions.address: Lyman Gilmore School, corner Gilmore Way and W. Main St, Grass Valley
decisions.description: Gilmore Flying Field. This windsock marks the site of one of the first flying fields in the United States. Here Lyman Gilmore, a contemporary of the Wright Brothers, pioneered in aviation by designing and building his own planes and gliders as early as 1898. Plaque on monument in front of school dedicated 1968 by Grand Lodge, Free and Accepted Masons: Lyman Gilmore, Jr. 1874-1951. Lyman Gilmore, Jr. established the first commercial airport on this site in 1907. To him and his memory the people of this community dedicate this school as the Lyman Gilmore School. Note: Gilmore did not use this field until the winter of 1911-12. As the Daily Transcript reported on January 29, 1912, "The principal place of business [of the Gilmore Airship Co.] was recently moved from Colfax to Grass Valley, and the meeting on Saturday evening was the first with the present board of directors." This pole was used originally at Gilmore Field, which Gilmore always claimed was the first commercial airport on the West Coast. The CETA mural on the north wall of the multipurpose building was painted in 1978.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Glasson House
decisions.description: This Victorian/Greek Revival house was built in 1866 for Miles P. O'Connor, who came to California in 1849 and to Nevada County in 1852. He served as justice of the peace for Little York township in 1852-53 and for Grass Valley 1862-69. He served one term in the state assembly and two in the state senate. He earned a fortune from the famous Idaho gold mine and was instrumental in creation of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. After selling this house to John Polglase in 1873, he moved to San Jose and funded various institutions, including a sanitarium later known as O'Connor Hospital. John Polglase, a hard-rock miner from Cornwall, came to Grass Valley in 1864. In 1875 Polglase bought an interest in John Glasson's grocery and provisions store while continuing to work underground in the Idaho mine. In 1882 he sold the house to his partner, and Glasson and his heirs owned it from then until 1973. John Glasson, like Polglase, came here in 1864 from England, and like O'Connor he played a prominent role in Nevada County history. He bought the local gas company in 1885-86 and in 1888 merged it with the Grass Valley Electric Light and Motor Co. He built the countyÃ
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Glenbrook Basin
decisions.address: Sutton Way, south of Brunswick Rd, next to bus shelter in Glenbrook Plaza parking lot
decisions.description: Early In the 1850's Glenbrook Basin first became known as the site of county fairs, horse racing and other special events. When Olympia Park, located at the south end of the basin, opened in 1901, Glenbrook Basin became the recreational center of Nevada County and much of Northern California, and remained so for over fifty years. People would come to the basin by horse and buggy or later the automobile, but those wishing for a little more excitement would often use the Nevada County Traction Companyâ€™s trolley, or the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. The large grandstands of the three-quarter-mile racetrack stood near the junction of present Brunswick Road and Sutton Way. Trotting, pacing, and running horse races were held at the track, and motor car races began in 1913. The racetrack was one of the first electrically lighted racetracks in the state. A dam was constructed just northeast of the existing electrical substation to form Lake Olympia. The lake soon became one of the BasinÃ
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Gold Quartz Discovery
decisions.address: Corner of Jenkins St and Hocking Ave
decisions.description: This tablet commemorates the discovery of gold-bearing quartz and the beginning of quartz mining in California. The discovery was made on Gold Hill by George McKnight October, 1850 The occurrence of gold-bearing quartz was undoubtedly noted here and elsewhere about the same time, or previously, but the above discovery created the great excitement that started the development of quartz mining into a great industry. The Gold Hill Mine from 1850 to 1857 is credited with a total production of $4,000,000.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Golden Era Lounge/309 Broad Street
decisions.description: Buildings on this site have housed bars and saloons since the Gold Rush era. Diana Saloon was opened in 1856. Stumpf's Miner's Hotel was established in 1863, Frank's Place (Eilerman) Saloon in 1887, and Costello & Jepson in 1910. The current building was constructed in 1886 following a major fire. W. B. Celio and H. L. Gove purchased the property in 1906, and it remained in the Celio family until 2015. Costello & Jepson established a "soda fountain" during the Prohibition years, followed by a series of bars that included the Success and Duffy's Success. Golden Era opened in 2015. The Brunswick-Balke-Collender Co.'s 'The Record' bar was built circa 1895. The front and back bars were installed in 1904 and 1915 respectively.
decisions.geocodedAddress: 309 Broad Street/Golden Era Lounge, Nevada City, CA,

decisions.name: Graniteville
decisions.address: No. Bloomfield-Graniteville Rd, across from house at No. 35177. 50 ft past gazebo bearing Graniteville sign.
decisions.description: A camp established in this area in 1850 was called Eureka; then later, Eureka South. By 1856 it sometimes had a winter population of 1000. Rich placer mining deposits were played out by 1865, but quartz mining continued. By 1867, total gold taken from this area was estimated at 20 million dollars. Area also important as distribution point for water from reservoirs, fed to hydraulic mines below by ditches. Name changed to Graniteville when Post Office was established on Aug. 26, 1867.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Grass Valley AME Church and School Site
decisions.address: 315-323 S. Church Street, Grass Valley. Plaque located across the street in front of 328 S. Church St.
decisions.description: "Across the street, on lots 315, 317, 319 and 323 S. Church Street, the Grass Valley African Methodist Episcopal Church and School were erected in 1854 and 1865 respectively by the local African American community. Reverend Peter Green and Church trustee and former slave Isaac Sanks, along with the other members of the congregation, worked to abolish slavery, secure voting rights for African American men, eliminate the ban on testimony of African Americans in court, and gain access to public accommodations and education. The AME Church was known for its exceptional choir and accomplished musicians. It was the site for fundraising events, literary readings, and the celebration of the Emancipation Proclamation. African American parents, whose children were denied entry into white schools, built their own schoolhouse next to the Church. Both the Church and School were sold in 1893 at public auction to Charles Clinch and the funds returned to the AME Church. Shortly thereafter, the new owners built the four Victorian homes that still stand on the property today."
