decisions.name: Grass Valley Historical District
decisions.description: Dedicated in 1969 by the Grass Valley City Planning Commission. Description: A colorful, old gold-mining town established in 1849 at Boston Ravine and later named Grass Valley by a company of emigrants. The atmosphere of early mining days still clings to the town. Significant sites include the Empire Mine State Historic Park, the North Star House and the homes of Lola Montez and Lotta Crabtree.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Grass Valley Masonic Lodge
decisions.description: Masonry in Grass Valley. Madison Lodge #23 was established under dispensation from the Grand Lodge of California on May 4th 1852. Its permanent charter was issued on May 5th 1853. Madison Lodge held its first two meetings at the old Columbus School on the present site of Hennessy School. The Lodge moved to a building on the south side of Main St. where it now meets Auburn St. This wooden building was destroyed along with most of Grass Valley in the great fire of September 12 and 13, 1855. A few days later, the Masons met with the Odd Fellows to select a site for a new brick building to house both organizations at 110 Mill St. This building was Madison's home for nearly 73 years, until fire destroyed the Masonic portion of the building in December 1928. For the next nine years the Lodge met in the auditorium of the old Strand Theater at 161 Mill St. During this time, planning was being completed for our present building, which saw its first meeting on January 29, 1938. For 150-years, in both good times and bad, through 7 wars and a great depression, the Masonic spirit of Brotherly Love, Relief and Truth has continued on through our brethren of honorable men of all backgrounds, professions and faith. This plaque was set on May 9, 2002 in rededication ceremonies conducted by C. Ray Whitaker, Grand Master of Masons in California and Elmer B. Curtis, Master of Madison Lodge.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Grass Valley Public Library (Josiah Royce Library)
decisions.description: There are four plaques. First plaque and fir tree dedicated 1931 by Native Daughters and Native Sons of the Golden West: 1854 - Himalayan Fir - 1916. Planted In Memory of Dr. Josiah Royce one of AmericaÃ
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Grass Valley's Famed Maple
decisions.address: Neal St, in private yard at corner of School St
decisions.description: This beautiful, Brandywine Maple, subject for artists and photographers, was brought from the fields of Gettysburg, and planted in 1876 by the widow of the poet and editor of the Grass Valley National newspaper, John Rollin Ridge. Surviving the fire which leveled the home, it now adorns the lovely Victorian home built by Mr. and Mrs. Charles Edward Clinch in 1900. Roy Clinch Tremoureux, grandson, notes proudly that many trees which beautify our town have come from this famed maple. Note: Time has ravaged the maple.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Hard Rock Gold Mining
decisions.address: Memorial Park, Grass Valley, near creek by Race St culvert
decisions.description: Hard Rock Gold Mining 1850-1957. Within a one-mile radius of this spot more than 500 million dollars worth of gold was produced by deep mines, including the Empire, North Star and the Idaho-Maryland. During that time this was the worldÃ
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Hartung House
decisions.description: In August of 1888 Nevada and Emma (Schwartz) Hartung purchased about 220 acres bordering Pleasant Valley Rd. and Piper Lane in Pleasant Valley (now Penn Valley). At the time of purchase, a small house and a barn were on the property. These two structures likely were built in the early 1860s when the property was owned by James and Hannah Saxon. The first house, still standing in the 1930â€™s, had deteriorated and was razed. The well, located in the front porch of the house remains. The barn now extended on both ends, still exists. The Hartungs built the current house in 1889. Pear, apple and walnut crops became the primary means of support for the family. A few of the original pear trees remain. Nevada August Hartung was born in August 1853 on the shores of Webber Lake, California in a wagon train on the family's way west from Illinois. It is reported that Nevada's given name was the result of the parentsâ€™ awe at their first sight of the Sierra Nevada Mountains. He grew up on his father Adolph Hartung's farm at Kentucky Flat and married Emma Schwartz in 1880. By the time of their 1888 purchase of the property, the Hartungs had three children, Frank (b.1880), Herman (b. 1884) and Mamie (b. 1886). The Hartungs bought the farm from Samuel and Agnes Yeo (owners 1888-1888) and prior to that the property was owned by Joseph Peers (1886-1888) and the Saxons (ca.1860-1886). In 1849-1850, John Rose and William Reynolds operated a trading post and cattle corral, both thought to be located on what became the Hartung property. The current owner, Pete Brennan, acquired the property in 1971 and operates a horse boarding facility.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Hirschman's Pond
decisions.description: "Nevada County's diverse gold rush population included a large Jewish community. Most were immigrants from Europe, attracted by the opportunity to make their fortunes and by the County's relative freedom and absence of discrimination against Jews. Many were merchants and many were miners. One of the more successful miners was Leb Hirschman (1823-1893), a native of Bavaria, who arrived here in the early 1850s, and mined around Oustamah Hill. In 1866, Hirschman, Grover & Co. purchased the American Hill Diggins, one of the the principal hydraulic mines in Nevada City, and operated it for many years. This pond, on the site of the mine, bears his name."

decisions.name: Hobart Mills Site
decisions.address: Just east of Hwy 89, five miles north of Truckee. There are two plaques, about 200 yards apart
decisions.description: First plaque dedicated 1938 by descendants of Walter Scott Hobart is about 0.2 m. north of the junction of Hobart Mills Rd with Old Hwy 89 on west side of Old 89: This monument marks the townsite of Hobart Mills, California. A logging and lumbering enterprise of sixty-six years life in the Sierra Nevada mountains it commemorates and honors its founder Walter Scott Hobart 1839-1892 and those whose loyalty, industry and ability made its successful operation possible.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Hobart Mills Site Second Plaque
decisions.description: Walter Scott Hobart's Sierra Nevada Wood & Lumber Company moved machinery, buildings and railroad equipment here from Lake Tahoe in 1896. The town and business flourished for 40 years, ceasing operations in 1936. Fibreboard Corporation operated a mill and railroad here from 1946 to 1955, logging timber purchased from Southern Pacific. The old mill was burned in 1964. Hobart Mills was an important lumbering center and a major contributor to the heritage of the Eastern Sierra.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Holbrooke Hotel
decisions.description: Holbrooke Hotel Founded 1851. Californiaâ€™s oldest hotel in continuous operation. Among its century-old archives are names of such notables as Presidents Grant, Harrison, Cleveland; authors Mark Twain, Bret Harte; boxers Corbett, Fitzsimmons; actor Gilbert Barry, and highwayman Black Bart. Present structure built in 1862 following infamous fire. Landmark registered and second plaque dedicated 1978 by State of California, CHL 914: Ã±The present bar of the hotel has been in continuous operation since 1852, when it was known as the Golden Gate Saloon. The wooden building had a rear extension called The Exchange Hotel and offered food and lodging by January 1853. The saloon was destroyed in the fire of 1855 and rebuilt out of fieldstone with a brick front. The Holbrooke, built in 1862, had the adjacent Golden Gate Saloon incorporated in the hotel building. Third plaque dedicated by Grass Valley Downtown Association: Presidential Square. These Presidents of the United States have visited this site: Ulysses S. Grant, James A. Garfield, Benjamin Harrison, Grover Cleveland, Herbert Hoover. Note: No evidence exists that some of these notables stayed at the Holbrooke or visited Grass Valley. Local lore has it that Gilbert Barry is a Clamper fabrication.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Hooligan Rock
decisions.address: 11200 Donner Pass Rd, east end of parking lot
decisions.description: Behind this broken rock, vigilante justice in the form of a tar and feather party or other form of physical punishment was meted out to the villains and ruffians who plagued Truckee through the dawn of the 20th-century. Constable Jacob Teeter bravely fought against vigilantism and his friend, attorney C. F. McGlashan, blasted the rock, ending its symbolism of terror, and used parts of the rock in a wall around his mansion. His granddaughter, Nona, recalled that the rock formed a 'backdrop of ugly secret scenes, floggings in the night, scalding tar and a smother of feathers, curses and screams for mercy.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Humbug/North Blomfield Cemetery
decisions.address: North Bloomfield Rd, west of North Bloomfield, Malakoff Diggins State Historical Park
decisions.description: "This 'hallowed place', that resides with in the boundaries of the Malakoff State Historical Park represents a 'tapestry of humanity.' Those who dared to carve out a place for themselves in an unknown and untamed wilderness. They came seeking riches, changing the landscape they encountered for all of time. They left behind a legacy entwined with turmoil, destruction, invention and lives lived hard. In 1855, Isreal Joiner, who was shot for stealing gold from a 'sluice box', and George Carter who lived to be 80, were the first to be buried in Humbug Cemetery. In 1857 when the town got its post office, the name was changed to North Bloomfield. George Edwards, John Enders, John McNulty, and Louise Miller; they were the first to be buried under the new banner, North Bloomfield Cemetery. By the end of the Nineteenth Century, as the mining played out, this town was all but dead. However, in an almost bazaar [sic] manner this cemetery has continued to grow! As of 1984 there were 170 recorded buried here from Austria, Australia, Bavaria, Canada, China, Denmark, England, France, Germany, Holland, Ireland, Portugal, Prussia, Scotland and America. In March 2017, Debra Phanner, a long time Park asst. was buried here. So, as you enter this portal into the past know that you are walking on 'hallowed ground' with many untold stories waiting to be discovered!"

decisions.name: Hunt's Hill (Gouge Eye)
decisions.address: About 6 m. east of Nevada City, off Buckeye Rd, about 1 m. from the intersection with Red Dog Rd
decisions.description: Hunt's Hill (also Gouge Eye) is a former mining camp founded in 1852 by a miner named Hunt. It was located on one of the deepest parts of the rich Blue Lead channel of gold-bearing gravel. In 1855, one of the mining claims established by some French miners, was â€œjumped.â€

decisions.name: Hydraulic Gate Valve
decisions.description: Hydraulic gold mining 21-inch gate valve 1880 Used at the You Bet-Red Dog Diggin's east of Nevada City periodically 1880- 1935 to control water flow from Chalk Bluff reservoir to the mine. Hydraulic nozzle mounted opposite was operated at that mine by Thomas B. Brady. Manufactured by Joshua Handy Works, San Francisco. Wt. 1,500 lbs. approx. A gift from the Brady Family.

decisions.name: Hydraulic Mining
decisions.address: Railroad Ave at back entrance to Northern Queen Inn
decisions.description: The first hydraulic mining originated here at American Hill Diggins by Edward Matteson in 1853. Matteson originally used canvas hose. The canvas hose was replaced with crinoline hose in 1860. These miners' giants, also known as water cannons, monitors and dictators, washed hillsides away with high pressure water, uncovering gold bearing beds. This mining technique flourished profitably until 1884. Rivers were being filled with mud and debris causing flooding of the rich farm lands in the valley. At that time, a lawsuit brought by farmers against hydraulic mining was heard in Marysville, California where a decision known as The Sawyer Decision stopped all mining. Under a special use permit, granted by the Department of Fish and Game, these hydraulic monitors were last used at the Pacific Slab Mine near Foresthill, California in 1970, which was documented by the California Department of Parks and Recreation.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Idaho Maryland Core Sample
decisions.description: Plaque dedicated 1961 by Historical Society: Core - Weight 11 Tons. Portion taken from Idaho Shaft No. 2 bored to a vertical depth of 1,125 feet, diameter of shaft five feet, the core boring barrel was invention of J. B. Newsom, mining engineer. Was used as an experiment by the Idaho Maryland Mine Corporation under general manager Errol MacBoyle. With the cooperation of the directors of the 17th District Agricultural Association, through their manager, Malcolm E. Hammill, this core was erected in July 1961. Note: The Newsom drilling machine bored a round shaft, using no explosives. It was cheaper because no timbering was needed. Similar cores are displayed at Calanan Park and Empire Mine Park.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Idaho-Maryland Mine Site
decisions.address: Hillside adjacent to Centennial Dr and Whispering Pines Lane, south of Idaho Maryland Rd
decisions.description: The Idaho quartz mine was discovered in 1863. It prospered under the Coleman brothers from 1867-93, then consolidated with the Maryland and other small mines. Eventually it became one of the 'Big Three' of the Northern Mines, the other two being the Empire and the North Star. Its final years under the management of Errol MacBoyle were very productive. He developed a revolutionary method of shaft sinking, using a huge core drill called the Newsom drilling machine. World War II and government order L208 brought about final closing. Note: Various parts of the mine still exist. The office building is off the east side of Centennial Drive, between Idaho Maryland Road and Whispering Pines Lane. A headframe is located near the Grass Valley Scrap, Iron & Metal yard. Learn more about the mine from Gold in Quartz: The Legendary Idaho Maryland Mine by Jack Clark, or from MacBoyle's Gold by Gage McKinney, both available from Comstock Bonanza Press.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Indian Medicine Stone
decisions.address: 621 E. Broad St, opposite N. Pine St
decisions.description: Pioneer Emigrant Trail. This boulder was known to the pioneers as the Indian Medicine Stone. On its top are hollows in which the Indians lay while taking sun baths to cure their ills. One branch of the Emigrant Trail leading from Truckee Pass to the gold mines of Nevada County and to the Valley of the Sacramento passed close beside this stone.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Isabel Hefelfinger Wagon Shed
decisions.description: The Wagon Shed. Generations of Californians living in the northern mines depended for their economics and social well-being on wheel transportation powered by the horse, mule, the ox. The vehicles displayed here represent some of those used to move people, goods and gold. The Nevada County Historical Society, Hydraulic Parlor No. 56, City of Nevada City, California Department of Parks & Recreation, Nevada City Lions Club and many other civic groups and individuals cooperated to preserve this collection. Second plaque dedicated 1993 by the Historical Society: Isabel Hefelfinger Wagon Shed. This building is dedicated to the memory of Isabel Ross Hefelfinger (1899-1991). She encouraged many by her interest and dedication in remembering the past. Her untiring efforts to preserve history of Nevada County will not be forgotten.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Jacob van Blaren
decisions.address: Just east of 19321 Wildflower Dr, Lake Wildwood
decisions.description: Anthony House Way Station, Pub & Tavern was located just off shore from Commodore Park. It was a stage stop between Marysville & the Virginia City Silver Mines. Jacob Van Blaren, a Civil War bugler, stopped at Anthony House for a period of time and died while staying there. He was in his forties at his death. Proprietors of Anthony House decided to bury him behind the building and it wasn't until Boise Cascade was in negotiations with the historical society that they named the area surrounding his burial site Bugler Park. It is uncertain at what point in Lake Wildwood's history that the park's name was changed to Commodore Park

decisions.name: Jax Truckee Diner
decisions.address: Corner of Bridge and W. River Sts.,
decisions.description: Originally built in October 1948 by Kullman Diner Co. Newark, New Jersey and shipped to West Chester, PA. known as the “Birmingham Grill” where it continually operated until purchased by the Carey Family in 1992. On July 26, 1995 the diner arrived in Truckee, California. No other diner of it's (sic) size and class had ever been trucked across the continent. After extensive restoration the diner was re-opened to the public on December 7, 1995.

decisions.name: Kentucky Flat School
decisions.description: he Kentucky Flat School was originally located near the Kentucky Flat mining camp below Kentucky Ridge along the old road to Bridgeport, today's Bitney Springs Road. Within a few years the mining camp's gold had been depleted and its residents moved on. In 1855, the Kentucky Flat School was moved to its present location at Newtown, two miles east of Kentucky Flat. The towns were two of the earliest established gold mining camps in Nevada County, both founded in 1850. Mr. Hubertus Schardin, who emigrated from Germany, named Newtown and is considered its founder. He established a store and market there in 1850 to supply the miners. On August 21, 1877 Mr. Schardin deeded to the Kentucky Flat School District the property the school now sits on. Tragically, in 1895 the original Kentucky Flat School was destroyed by fire. It was quickly rebuilt the same year. The School continued to operate for 108 years, until 1963 when it was closed after proudly serving generations of Nevada County school children. The School was annexed to the Grass Valley School District in July 1964 and leased to the Kentucky Flat Community Center. Today, in 2022 it continues to serve young children from surrounding areas as the Kentucky Flat Community Pre-school
decisions.reviewNotes: US Census Geocoder result accepted as a free, interpolated street-address coordinate; not storefront/building certainty.

decisions.name: Kentucky Ridge Mine Site
decisions.description: During gold rush days, Nevada County had a significant Black population. Most were free, but even though California had entered the Union as a free state, some were enslaved before the Civil War. From 1851-1852, the Kentucky Ridge Mine was the largest enterprise in California operated by slave labor. Gold was first discovered on this property in early 1851. By October of 1851, Colonel William F. English brought with him from Florida and South Carolina about 40 slaves to operate the gold mine. He was one of over a dozen slave owners in Nevada County. The mine failed after English died in a gun accident on August 27, 1852, and the Black mine workers gradually moved into the towns. One of them, Isaac Sanks, the husband of Chasey, one of the slaves, became a religious leader, businessman and political figure who helped gain Blacks the right to serve on juries and the right to vote by 1870. He died at his home in Grass Valley on May 9, 1894

decisions.name: Kohler Building
decisions.address: Washington Rd, next to hotel in town of Washington
decisions.description: Built by Henry Kohler, pioneer merchant and miner born in Germany in 1825. He came to Washington from Indiana in 1854. He mined until 1861 when he opened a general merchandise store to supply groceries, hardware and mining supplies to the area.

decisions.name: Kruger-White House
decisions.description: Designated a National Historic Landmark 1982: This property Kruger-White House has been placed on the National Register of Historic Places by the United States Department of the Interior. Note: W. H. Kruger, a lumberman and entrepreneur, built this impressive home 107 years ago. Mr. Kruger contributed significantly to the 19th century community development of Truckee as one of the town's leading commercial pioneers. Mr. C. B. White, a banker, bought the house in 1904. Today, the Kruger-White home stands as an historic architectural landmark in a town which, for over a century, has represented an important gateway to California.

decisions.name: Lake Tahoe Railway & Transportation Company Site
decisions.address: Just east of 10130 W. River St, across the railroad tracks from Amtrak station. The plaque is missing. If you have any information about its whereabouts, please contact the publisher.
decisions.description: The Lake Tahoe Railway & Transportation Company was incorporated on December 1898 by Duane Leroy Bliss. The Narrow Gauge Railroad opened for seasonal traffic on May 1, 1900. The line ran from the Southern Pacific Depot in Truckee, 15 miles south to the shores of Lake Tahoe. Tahoe City hosted the railroadâ€™s shops. The Tahoe Tavern hotel and pier where the train met the Bliss steamer - Tahoe. Southern Pacific Purchased the line and converted it to standard gauge in 1926. Passenger Service operated until abandonment in November 1943.

decisions.name: Lindeboom Memorial
decisions.description: Plaque dedicated 1986 by Veterans of Foreign Wars Post 2655: Dedicated to the memory of Tina Lindeboom March 1911-February 1984. With gratitude and appreciation of the American people for gallant service in assisting Allied soldiers escape from the enemy W.W. II 1939-1945.

decisions.name: Lone Grave
decisions.address: Hwy 20, 8 m. east of Nevada City
decisions.description: Julius Albert Apperson Born June 1855-Died May 6,1858. A pioneer who crossed the plains to California who died and was buried here. The Emigrant Trail followed along this ridge and through Nevada City. The marking of this lone grave perpetuates the memory of all lone graves throughout the state of California. Plaque incorrectly identifies this Landmark as NEV 001. Note: Julius Apperson was not a passerby. The boy died as the result of burns sustained on his parents' ranch and he was buried on Apperson property. The original date on the grave was May 31,1858. There are two white wooden grave markers nailed to trees behind the fenced-in gravesite. One says "In memory of Julius Albert Apperson, died May 6, 1858, age 2 years, 2 mos., 25 days." The other contains the boy's initials and dates. A gravestone on the ground inside the fence reads: In Loving Memory of Julius Albert Apperson, Died May 6, 1858, Age 2 yrs, 2 mos, 25 days." However, if that was his true age he would have been born in February 1856 instead of June 1855, as the plaque states. References; (1) Testimony of John Milton Apperson, a Tehama County cattleman and older brother of Julius, on February 25, 1936; (2) "Lone Graves of Nevada County" in the Nevada County Historical Society Bulletin, January 1961; (3) an item in the Nevada Democrat, May 5, 1858, stating that the child was terribly burned near the house where he lived on April 30.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Loutzenheiser Pharmacy Building/Foothill Flowers
decisions.description: Operated by pioneer druggist William Loutzenheiser (1824_1905) and son, John G. (1861-1929). The pharmacy begin here in a wooden building in 1851. The structure gave way to the present brick edifice in the fall of 1855. William was a member of the 1861 city Board of Trustees, one of the original locators of the Idaho Quartz claim, and was active in several other mining ventures. On July 4, 1887 the pharmacy was the site of a bloody duel between two Grass Valley merchants, Henry Sylvester and John Davis, in which the latter was killed. In 1928 the business changed hands after 77 years of continuous operation by the Loutzenheiser family. Between 1928 and 1966 the building had many tenants. In the fall of 1966 Marie Johnson, known as the Flower Lady, founded Foothill Flowers. The interior of the building was destroyed by fire in May 1984 and was subsequently remodeled.

decisions.name: Martin Luther Marsh House
decisions.description: Martin Luther Marsh arrived in Nevada City in 1851 after a journey from Ohio by the Nicaragua route. He worked as a carpenter, left to mine at Iowa Hill and finally returned to enter the lumber business. With his brother Dan he set up a saw mill on Little Deer Creek (now Pioneer Park). The enterprise boomed and in 1873 Marsh built this magnificent home using carefully seasoned sugar pine for the interior woodwork. His granddaughter Lucille and her late husband James B. Christie restored the house in 1955.

Truckeeâ€™s Patriarch, Historian, Author, Editor, Attorney, Legislator, Inventor, Entomologist and Astronomer. His last public address was given in 1926 at the Donner Summit Bridge Dedication. This site is named in his memory by the people of Truckee.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: McGuashen Butterfly Collection *
decisions.description: "Charles Fayette McGlashan, noted author of History of the Donner Party: A Tragedy of the Sierra, started his butterfly collection in 1872. In the early 1900s, his daughter, Ximena, joined him in collecting and arranging the Lepidoptera collection. Ximena, then referred to as The Butterfly Princess, was instrumental in growing the collection and started her own business, The Butterfly Farm. She continued this venture until she attended college. With her father, she also wrote and published The Butterfly Farmer, a monthly periodical. Their collection included up to 20,000 specimens, and they were granted a number of entomological mounting patents. First housed in the original Rocking Stone Tower, the collection was moved several times and finally moved here in 2015."

decisions.address: Behind the Old Jail, northeast corner of Spring and Jibboom Sts
decisions.description: First plaque: "THE ARCH Erected on the entrance of the old Masonic building in 1909. Destroyed by a gas explosion in 1993. Dedicated to the Town of Truckee - October 27, 2007 by Truckee Lodge #200 F&AM Gary E. Miner, W.M. Dedicated to Truth, Equality & Freedom." Second plaque: "Celebrating 150 years of Freemasons in Truckee. Lodge #200 established on October 14, 1869. Dedicated to the Town of Truckee October 14, 2019 by Truckee Lodge #200 F&AM Christopher Rochefort, W.M. Dedicated to Truth, Equality & Freedom." Note: The Arch is all that remains of the historic Masonic Lodge # 200 building, built in 1909 and destroyed by fire and explosion in 1993. It reportedly was the only lodge to have had a chiseled, Sierra granite entrance-way, which was preserved after the fire. In 2007, the entrance-way Arch was reconstructed and placed on the grounds of the Truckee-Donner Historical Society's Old Jail Museum.

decisions.name: Memorial Grove Nevada City
decisions.description: This grove honors those who served our country in the armed forces. It contains five plaques. Plaque dedicated 1946 by the citizens of Nevada City: This plot of land set aside by the City of Nevada, these trees living tributes to our war dead. This plaque and flag pole erected by the citizens of Nevada City dedicated July 4, 1946 to the memory of our heroes who gave their lives in World War II that the American way of life may continue. Caswell Fisher, Tevis Ferguson, Merton Downey, Donald Osrey, Bert Graham, Fred Doby, Eugene Davies, Albert Conti, Ernest Chapman, William G. Bennett, Hans G. Hirsef, Clifford M. Ruth, Timothy R. Livesay, Virgil Roseborough, David McCreary, Eugene Rickard, Robert D. Proctor, Louis G. Netz, Glenn Ponticelli , Walter O. St. John, Carl D. Tobiassen, Gus Wasley, James M. Odegaard, William Pohley, Reid Shurtleff. Plaque dedicated 1990 by Banner Mountain Post No. 2655, Veterans of Foreign Wars: Korea, June 25, 1950 - July 27, 1953, 'The Forgotten War - Remembered.' Let us not forget those Americans who served, those who died and those who still remain on that foreign soil in defense of freedom. As a result of that war: 54,246 Americans were killed 8,177 Americans are missing in action 389 Americans are prisoners of war. Plaque dedicated 1992 by Banner Mountain Post No. 2655, Veterans of Foreign Wars: Vietnam. All gave some - some gave all. If you are able, save for them a place inside of you and save one, backward glance when you are leaving for the places they can no longer, go. Be not ashamed to say you loved them, though you may or may not, have always. Take what they have left and what they have taught you, with their dying and keep it with your own. And in that time when men, decide and feel safe to call the war insane, take one moment to, embrace those gentle heroes you left behind. - Major Michael Davis O'Donnell, 1 January 1970, Dak To, Vietnam. Note: Major O'Donnell was a helicopter pilot. On 24 March 1970, while attempting to rescue eight soldiers, his chopper was shot down. He and, three crew members were declared missing in action. This memorial plaque is dedicated as a tribute to those who served and fought in the Vietnam War, especially those who died, and those yet to return. Plaque dedicated 1995 by Banner Mountain Post No. 2655, Veterans of Foreign Wars: POW MIA You are not forgotten. Plaque dedicated 2007 by the citizens of Nevada County: War on Terror We Will not Forget. In honor of the Men and Women who have served and will serve in the ongoing Global War on Terror and to those Valiant Few who have given their lives, therein... . We, the citizens of Nevada County, dedicate this memorial. L/Cpl Adam Strain, 20, L/Cpl John 'J.T.' Lucente, 19, 1st Lt. Nathan Krisoff, 25, Cpl Sean Stokes, 24, Ssg David P. Senft, 27. There are also memorial plaques for the following Vietnam veterans located on the following Golden Center Freeway overpasses around Nevada City. PfcUSA: Philip A. Tritsch 06/10/1943-01/28/1969, west end Washington St SP/4 101st ABN: Ronald J. Walber 06/3/1948-04/25/1968, west end Broad St Michael D. Goeller 06/2/1948-06/01/1969, east end Sacramento St Capt USA: John Stuart Seeley 04/10/1932-06/27/1966, west end Gold Flat Rd 1st Lt USA: James F

decisions.name: Mt. Elisha Stephens
decisions.address: Parking lot next to Donner Summit Bridge on Donner Pass Rd, Old Hwy 40
decisions.description: Stephens-Townsend-Murphy Party of 1844 First Pioneer Wagons Over the Sierra Nevada. On May 22, 1844, this small wagon party of 50 men, women and children 'jumped off' from Council Bluffs, Iowa, bound for California. These courageous pioneers were the first emigrants to take wagons over the Sierra Nevada, opening the Truckee Route of the California Trail. The Murphy, Townsend, Miller, and Hitchcock families formed the main groups of the party. Caleb Greenwood was the hired guide, and Elisha Stephens was elected Captain of the wagon train. After following the Oregon Trail to Fort Hall, Idaho, they turned away from the well-worn path and headed west into the unfamiliar Nevada desert. A Paiute Indian Chief showed them how to reach a river flowing from the Sierra Nevada, which they named 'Truckee' in his honor. Overcoming severe conditions, they followed the Truckee River to the base of the Sierra. Six members, on horseback, continued to follow the Truckee River and eventually arrived safely at Sutter's New Helvetia on December 10, 1844. Six of the wagons were left in the charge of two men, and young Moses Schallenberger, near Truckee Lake (present Donner Lake). Near this spot, with two feet of snow on the ground, they worked the remaining five wagons across the summit of the mighty Sierra Nevada on November 25, 1844. Deepening snow forced them to camp near Big Bend, on the South Yuba River. Leaving the wagons with two men and the women and children, the remaining 17 men continued on to New Helvetia to seek help. Somehow, John Sutter persuaded the men to enlist in his forces taking part in a Mexican civil war. Eventually the families were reunited, and all members (including two babies born on the way) arrived safely at Sutterâ€™s Fort in early March 1845. Note: Mount Elisha Stephens is west of bridge on north side of road.

Under the Sisters of Mercy it served from 1866 to 1932 as the first orphanage of the Northern Mines. It functioned as an academy (1868-1965) and a convent (1866-1968).

decisions.name: Nathaniel P. Brown House
decisions.description: Nathaniel P. Brown, founding owner of the Nevada Daily Transcript and early political figure in Nevada City, lived here from 1865 until 1909. He expanded the original cottage to its current size in the 1880s, allowing for frequent entertaining. The home was built in 1858 by Jesse S. Wall, an early settler and merchant. Nathaniel Brown came to Nevada City from Massachusetts in 1853 at the age of 18, following his friend Aaron Sargent. He was part owner of the weekly Nevada Journal from 1855 to 1859. In 1860, Brown founded the countyÃ

decisions.name: National Exchange Hotel
decisions.description: Originally the Bicknell Block, stagecoach, mail, express and telegraph center in mining days. Companies which became the Pacific Gas & Electric formed here. Site of first whipping post in state. Facade unchanged since 1854. This hotel, one of the oldest in continuous operation west of the Rockies, opened for business in August, 1856. Originally known as the 'Bicknell Block,' the structure consists of three common-walled buildings of simple brick construction embellished with classic revival and wrought iron railing ornamentation.

Hieronimus & family brewed and served lager beer to Nevada City, Queen of the Northern Mines and hydraulic mining communities. Note: This brewery was erected on the site of Temperance Hall after that building burned. After his brewery on Searls Avenue (See 102) burned in 1893, Simon Hieronimus brewed his beer in this building until he died in 1911.

decisions.name: Nevada City AME Church Site
decisions.description: "On this site in 1864, the African Methodist Episcopal Church was dedicated by Bishop T.M.D. Ward to serve Nevada City's Black community, many of whom lived nearby and had been attending services in Grass Valley at the AME church established there in 1854. The church's founders were Dennis Drummond Carter, Samuel Rodgers, Elijah Booth, John Hamilton, and James Jenkins. The original building was destroyed in a storm in 1865, but was soon rebuilt by the community. In addition to holding religious services, the church was a center for social, cultural, political and civil rights events, such as the "festivals of freedom" that commemorated the anniversary of the Emancipation Proclamation. The building also housed a "Sabbath School" established in 1867. The church was used until 1894, by which time the membership had dwindled and the property was sold and became a series of private residences. The proceeds from the sale of the property were returned to the AME Church."

decisions.name: Nevada City Hall
decisions.description: First plaque dedicated 1937 by Native Sons of the Golden West: Dedicated June 19,1937 to the Pioneer Councilmen who erected the first City Hall Felix Gillet, K. Casper, J. C. Locklin, Alec. Gault, Dan. Baker and to H. D. Shearer and Wm. H. Kilroy Officers who were killed in line of duty.... Second plaque (on floor of porch): This building dedicated to Truth-Liberty-Toleration by the Native Sons of the Golden West June 19,1937.Third plaque dedicated 1987 by Native Sons of the Golden West: U. S. Constitution. 1787 Bicentennial Nevada City. A Bicentennial Community. Fourth plaque dedicated 2000 by Nevada City Council: On February 14, 2000 a contract was executed, authorizing the completion of work that began in 1937. On that date, the City Council inaugurated a process by which a small, historically significant City Hall was transformed into a spacious and efficient public facility. Expansion and renovation have been fruitful to the original architectural style and designed to serve the community far in to the 21st century.... Dedicated to all who came before us and all who will follow. Fifth plaque dedicated 2000 by Nevada City Council: On the occasion of the dedication of a new City Hall at this location on December 19, 2000, the citizens and City Council of Nevada City humbly submit for posterity a time capsule secured in a concrete vault beneath this marker. Contained within are artifacts and documents representative of life in Nevada City as we prepare to embrace the uncharted future. We respectfully request this repository remain undisturbed until April 17, 2150, at which time Nevada City will celebrate its 300th birthday.

decisions.name: Nevada City Jewish Cemetery

decisions.description: The Nevada City Jewish Cemetery was established in 1854. The first burial was in 1856 and the last in 1890. Twenty nine headstones are visible, including that of Louis W. Dreyfuss, a cousin of Captain Alfred Dreyfus of France, the victim in the infamous Dreyfus Affair. The cemetery was restored around 50 years ago by the Commission for the Preservation of Pioneer Jewish Cemeteries and Landmarks. It is locked and fenced to protect against vandalism. Note: For more information see A Traveler's Guide to Pioneer Jewish Cemeteries of the California Gold Rush by Susan Morris.

decisions.name: Nevada City Masonic Lodge
decisions.description: The year was 1850 at a location not far from here. The first Masonic Lodge meeting was held under a Charter from state of Wisconsin. Lafayette Lodge #29 was born, the years following in 1851 & 1863 a major part of which was to be known as Nevada City was destroyed by fires. By this time California was a State and a new Lodge of California Charter was established as Nevada #13 F&AM in 1851. At this location the first masonry structure was built in 1852 as a store. The 1863 fire destroyed this building and again the Lodge at a different place. This site was purchased for $1,650 and rebuilt as a store with Lodge rooms up stairs. The Lodge moved in 1864. A dining room/office extension was built to the right in 1901. Over the past 136 years other Masonic units/orders have also been meeting here. Royal Arch, Nevada Chapter #6, Council #62, K. T Commandery #6, Evangeline Eastern Star #9. In honor of 150 years continuous Nevada Lodge #13 F&AM Masonic Actives. Grand Master of California Alvin J. Weis placed this plaque in rededication of this Lodge April 22, 2000. Note: The plaque is quoted verbatim; all errors are in the original.

decisions.description: Nevada County Courthouse History. Nevada County organized out of Yuba County May 11,1851. Wooden court-house and log jail built on Broad Street in 1851. The first courthouse on this site was erected in 1856. The building was damaged by fire in July of the same year and rebuilt. Destroyed by fire in 1863 and rebuilt in 1864. Remodeled and enlarged in 1900. Modernized and enlarged 1936-37, George C. Sellon, Architect, Lindgren and Swinerton, Inc., Contractors, Robert Steger, Building Superintendent.

decisions.name: Nevada County Narrow Gauge (III)
decisions.address: Railroad Ave at back entrance to Northern Queen Inn
decisions.description: For 66 years, the Nevada City depot served as the northern terminus for the railroad affectionately known as the 'Never Come Never Go.' In 1874 a group of 7 men, led by lumber mill and gold mine owner John C. Coleman, organized the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. Engineer John Flint Kidder was hired on January 14, 1875 to build a 23-mile narrow gauge railroad from the Central Pacific Railroad connection in Colfax to Grass Valley and Nevada City. On a snowy May 20, 1876, John Coleman drove the last spike in Nevada City to dedicate the completion of the railroad. John Kidder controlled the railroad from January 1884, until his death on April 10, 1901; the following day, his widow Sara was elected president of the railroad. Sara Kidder successfully managed the company until its sale on April 24, 1913 to the Oakland, Antioch and Eastern, a predecessor to the Western Pacific. On May 13, 1926, the N.C.N.G.R.R. was purchased by a group of local investors and it operated until December 1942. The scrapping of the railroad was completed by May 5,1943. Two other plaques at this location were dedicated on June 11,1994, but cannot presently be located. Text of second plaque: In fond appreciation of the known surviving employees of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad Co. 1876-1942. Bob Paine, Tony Dellea, Sadie Angelini, Eugene Katzer, Homer Bevared, George Jefferson, Edward Smith, Bill Tremewan. Text of third plaque: In grateful appreciation to the Northern Queen Inn and the Ramey family for their dedicated efforts in the restoration and preservation of Nevada Countyâ€™s railroad history.
