decisions.address: End of Flume St off Hwy 49
decisions.description: Erected in 1857 at a cost of $1500, the structure Is the earliest existing church in the county. It contains early furnishings. Second plaque dedicated 1980 by the church board and Chap 10 E Clampus Vitus: North San Juan United Methodist Church 1856. One of the oldest of its faith in continuous operation in California. Escaped three major fires which destroyed the town. The Rev. H. B. Sheldon, first assigned pastor, rode circuit from Brandy City to French Corral on horseback.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: North San Juan Protestant Cemetery
decisions.description: The North San Juan Protestant Cemetery is an unusually well preserved and beautiful cemetery and has served as a place of interment since 1851. It holds the remains of hundreds of individuals, with many life stories related to the Gold Rush. The grave monuments within its boundary are exceptionally varied in design and represent fine examples of gravestone art and symbolism. Situated in a lovely tree covered setting, it is one of the best examples of nineteenth century burial customs and history.
decisions.reviewNotes: No Diana coordinate and no confident ArcGIS fallback. Address-bearing rows are eligible for the optional free Census Geocoder pass.

decisions.name: North Star House
decisions.description: This 1905 structure was built for Arthur D. Foote, manager of the North Star mine from 1895 to 1913, and his wife, Mary Hallock Foote, a noted 19th-century author and illustrator. The structure was designed by Julia Morgan, Californiaâ€™s first licensed female architect and designer of the Hearst properties at San Simeon, Asilomar in Pacific Grove, and numerous buildings on the U. C. Berkeley campus, and in total, over 700 buildings. The property is representative of the era that brought Nevada County to the forefront of gold production in California.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: North Star Mine Powerhouse and Nevada County Traction Company
decisions.address: Allison Ranch Rd at McCourtney Rd
decisions.description: The North Star Powerhouse, built by A. D. Foote in 1895, was the first complete water powered compressed air transmission plant of its kind. Compressed air, generated by Pelton water wheels, furnished power for the entire mine operation. The 30-foot Pelton wheel was the largest in the world, and was in continuous use for over 30 years. Second plaque dedicated 1991 by American Society of Mechanical Engineers: International Mechanical Engineering Heritage Collection Pelton Water Wheel Collection North Star Power House Museum. The Pelton Wheel uses the momentum of a water jet impinging on buckets attached to the periphery of a wheel to produce power. It is a development of the primitive, so-called 'Hurdy Gurdy' wheel used in the California goldfields in the nineteenth century. Various inventors have contributed this type of water turbine, including Lester A. Pelton (1831-1908), who in 1880 patented the characteristic split bucket form. Subsequent important contributions to Pelton-Wheel technology were made by W. A. Doble. This collection illustrates the origin and development of the Pelton Waterwheel. American Society of Mechanical Engineers-1991.Third plaque dedicated 1997 by Historical Society near smaller wheel: Pelton Type Water Wheel. Produced 71000 hp at 327 rpm, diameter 10 ft. Weight 26,000 lbs. Built by Allis Chalmers of single cast nickel steel. Gift of Oroville-Wyandotte Irrigation Dist. Note: The building presently houses the Historical Society's Mining Museum.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Old Auburn Road Bridge (Site)
decisions.address: Old Auburn Rd, southeast of modern bridge over Wolf Creek about 4 m. south of McCourtney Rd
decisions.description: The Old Auburn Bridge was registered as a landmark in 1996 by Nevada County. The bridge consisted of a single span, pin-connected steel pony truss, 66-feet in length, 11-feet in width, with a timber deck carrying a single traffic lane. It was built in 1897 by Cotton Brothers and Co. of Oakland, California. In 2004, the old bridge was dismantled by the County as unsafe and replaced by a modern span. Recognizing the historical significance of the spot, the County erected a monument containing a bronze plaque: Historic Marker Perrin Crossing. This bridge replaces the original steel truss span constructed in 1897 and remained in continuous service until 2004. This crossing received its name from Joseph and Susan Perrin, who settled nearby in 1851. Close to this crossing the Perrin family operated a stagecoach stop known for its hospitality. This road was part of the original stagecoach route between Sacramento and Grass Valley. Note: Remains of the stagecoach stop can be seen about 100 ft. south of the monument
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Old Bear River Bridge
decisions.address: Hwy 174 at Bear River, between Colfax and Chicago Park
decisions.description: Since 1852 bridges near this site have served as the gateway to western Nevada County. This 1924 span was the first concrete bridge at this site and features a Roman style single arch design, constructed in a continuous pour method. The Colfax Highway Association led a citizens group that saved the bridge from the wreckerâ€™s ball in 1986, and placed this monument in 1992.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Old Brick Gothic Church Building
decisions.address: Corner of Main and Church Sts
decisions.description: Historic Old Brick Gothic Building. Constructed as a Congregational Church in 1857. Restored by First Baptist Church. Notes: Also site of Nevada Cityâ€™s pioneer nondenominational church, which burned in March 1851. It was replaced by a wooden Congregational Church, that burned in 1856. Cornerstone of this brick building was laid on July 4, 1858 (not 1857.) The roof and interior burned in 1863 and the church was rebuilt once more in 1864. The University of California was born at the church in 1853, when Henry Durant took part in a convention of Congregationalists and Presbyterians who resolved to establish a college in California. Durant soon opened the Contra Costa Academy in Oakland, that become the College of California then relocated to Berkeley as the University of California.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Old Capitol Building
decisions.description: The Old Capitol. Erected in 1870. In its heyday it was the social center of Truckee . . . Many of the leading thespians . . . politicians . . . and other celebrities of that era gathered here . . . Note: This plaque has disappeared. A plaque placed on the front of building by unknown persons reads: Capitol Building 1870 Truckee California. The first brick building in Truckee, it was originally Hurd's Saloon, then the Capitol Saloon, later used by the Piper Opera Company and the County Court.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Old Chinese Herb Shop
decisions.address: Corner of S.E. River St. and Brockway Rd.
decisions.description: Old Chinatown. Construction of the Central Pacific Railroad over Donner Summit brought thousands of Chinese laborers here in 1868. They used â€˜squatter's rights' to build a ramshackle colony along the Truckee River on what is now River Avenue East. A huge fire destroyed Chinatown on May 29, 1875, but the Orientals rebuilt their community. Finally, on Nov. 18, 1878, a group of about 500 citizens, under the leadership of the Caucasian League, tore down the shanty town and ordered the 1000 or more Chinese to leave. They simply moved across the river, adjoining this former site of a Chinese herb shop, and built anew. A third fire, on Jan. 3, 1883, consumed the 'new' Chinatown and brought about the gradual end to the Chinese colony here. Bronze plaque dedicated 1980 by Chap 3691 E Clampus Vitus. Text of bronze plaque: Old Chinese Herb Shop.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Old North San Juan School
decisions.address: 29190 Hwy 49, south end of North San Juan
decisions.description: The first school building on this site was moved from Flume Street in 1869. It burned in 1924, and this building was moved here from Lake Vera. This site operated as a school from 1869 until 1984. It is currently used as a community center and library.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Old Pacific Fruit Packing Shed
decisions.address: Mount Olive and Lower Colfax Rds, Chicago Park
decisions.description: A smaller shed, built here in 1926, collapsed during a snowstorm in 1929. Its successor was built in 1930 by the Civilian Conservation Corps. The Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad tracks, depot, and water tower were alongside this building, where peaches and pears from the surrounding orchards were packed and sent to Colfax by train. There the boxes were transferred to standard sized refrigerator freight cars to be shipped to the East.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Old Stone Garage
decisions.address: Southwest corner Jibboom and Bridge Sts
decisions.description: Truckeeâ€™s Old Stone Garage and Site of Townâ€™s First Dwelling. In 1863, Joseph Gray built Truckee's first structure on this site for use as the Dutch Flat-Donner Lake Wagon Road's toll station. The old log structure was moved in 1907 to the Englehart property on Church Street, one block east of this site, where it still stands and the Brethren of ECV dedicated a plaque in 1965. In 1909, D. J. Smith, with the help of European-trained stone masons, constructed the present building for use as a livery and garage.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Original Ott Assay Office Site
decisions.description: Site of the Original Ott Assay Office. J. J. Ott's assay in 1859 helped develop the great Comstock Lode and the State of Nevada. Note: Ottâ€™s original Assay Office was built of wood. It burned in the fire of 1863 and Ott moved into the existing brick building next door.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Ormonde Town Site
decisions.address: Maybert Rd, 3.6 m. east of Washington. About 3/10 m. past Maybert (Canyon Creek) bridge in Golden Quartz campground. Maybert Rd does not require 4-wheel-drive but not advised for cars with low clearance.
decisions.description: The town of Ormonde was established here in 1887. Downstream, across the mouth of Canyon Creek was Canyonville 1850, and upriver approximately four miles was Maybert 1886. These towns were part of the upper Washington Mining District and were the furthest upstream of the serious mining ventures along the South Yuba. During its brief history the district yielded 17 million dollars in gold, but by 1897 it was nearly totally abandoned. The remains have since been obliterated by flooding. Note: The town consisted of 8-10 buildings, including a store, saloon and boarding house.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Overland Emigrant Trail--Highway 49
decisions.address: Hwy 49 south of Grass Valley, 1.2 m. north of Combie Rd between Clivus Dr and Brewer Rd at South Wolf Creek bridge
decisions.description: At this point, the old Overland Emigrant Trail approaches the present highway. More than 100-years ago it resounded to the creaking wheels of pioneer wagons and the cry of hardy but footsore travelers buoyed by the realization that their long trip to the goldfields was about over.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Pelton Water Wheel
decisions.description: No. 4, 1928-1987. Produced 18,000 HP at 257 RPM and enough electrical power for 16,000 households. 12 ft. diameter, weight 15 tons. Built in San Francisco by Pelton Company. A Constitutional Bicentennial gift to Nevada City by Pacific Gas and Electric.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Pine Street Crossing at Nevada City
decisions.address: S. Pine St at Deer Creek
decisions.description: Pine Street Bridge 1996. Third span of Deer Creek at this location. It is a replica of the single arch steel Gault bridge (1903-1996) which replaced the suspension bridge (1862-1903) built by Andrew S. Hallidie, who later built the San Francisco cable car system. Note: The bridge is officially named the South Pine Street Bridge at Hallidie Crossing. Hallidie's patented structure collapsed two weeks after it opened, killing two men. The Gault bridge, similar to the one at Edwards Crossing (No. 128) was named for Nevada City Mayor Alexander Gault.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Pioneer Cemetery
decisions.address: East side of W. Broad St, near Hwy 49
decisions.description: This cemetery was established in 1851 on a knoll back of the First Methodist Church. This gave the site its name. Many noted citizens rest here. Note: The first cemetery in Nevada County after arrival of the 49ers. Located in the churchyard of the first Methodist Church that once occupied this site, it has been in use since 1851. A wooden marker was placed in 1949. The plaque is on a stone monument in the front part of the cemetery. Many famous pioneers and early settlers are buried here, including Henry Meredith and William Alphonso Sutter, the son of General John Sutter. Aaron A. Sargent's cenotaph is here though his remains were cremated and scattered by air above the Quaker Hill Mine, which he used to own.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Pioneer Emigrant Trail (I)
decisions.address: On Airport Rd about 100 feet from junction with Lake Vera Rd
decisions.description: A branch of the Pioneer Emigrant Trail leading from Truckee Pass to the gold mines of Nevada County and to the Valley of the Sacramento divided on Harmony Ridge near the site of Lone Grave. One route circled the north horizon to Blue Tent, down to Selby Flat, up through this gap and on south.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Pioneer Emigrant Trail (II)
decisions.address: 10867 Coyote St, near North Bloomfield Rd
decisions.description: A branch of the Pioneer Emigrant Trail leading from Truckee Pass to the gold mines of Nevada County traversed Harmony Ridge down through this gap and on to the Valley of the Sacramento.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Pioneers Memorial
decisions.address: Memorial Park, corner of Race St and Colfax Ave
decisions.description: Plaque dedicated 1929 by Native Daughters of the Golden West: In memory of our pioneer mothers & fathers dedicated June 12, 1929 by Manzanita Parlor No 29 N.D.G.W.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Plaza Grocery
decisions.description: This store, opened in 1882 by Tom Shurtleff, contained the only water-powered elevator in Nevada County. Longest continuous operation of any Nevada City grocery until it closed in 1973. Note: Building was a boarding house from 1880 to 1882. Celio family bought it in 1910 and leased to Scheemers in 1928. John Sbaffi bought the business in 1949 and purchased the building in 1965. It was a grocery until 1973, when 'The Jacks' leased it for use as a restaurant. It has been used primarily as a bar or restaurant since.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Pollard's Station
decisions.address: Near intersection of S. Shore Dr and Donner Pass Rd, west of Truckee
decisions.description: On this site, Joseph Delos Pollard constructed the first resort on the west end of Donner Lake. Between mid 1864 and early 1868 Pollardâ€™s Station on the Dutch Flat & Donner Lake Wagon Road was office to the Pioneer Stage Co. and Wells, Fargo & Co. Express, as well as a hotel, boasting bar room, reading room, billiards and a bowling alley. Visitors could rent boats, horses or carriages to enjoy the scenic grandeur. The station was ravaged by fire twice and rebuilt once. The station was no longer needed due to the completion of the railroad in June 1868, which put a virtual end to wagon and stage traffic across the Sierra.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Powell House
decisions.address: Southeast corner Pine and Spring Sts
decisions.description: Built in 1855 as the edifice of the Baptist Church. It was here that famed soprano Emma Nevada (Emma Wixom Palmer) made her debut at the age of three. In 1886 the church was remodeled as the residence of the E. T. R. Powell family. Note: The building now houses apartments.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Preston and Louisa Alexander Homesite
decisions.description: Preston Alexander, one of Nevada City's earliest African American settlers, purchased this property along Buckeye Ravine on May 19, 1859. The property was occupied by Preston (1834-1889), his wife, Louisa (1838-1902), and their five children, John, Mary Amelia, Horatio Henry, Louisa, and Rosella Elizabeth, until 1902. The Alexander family was well respected and highly regarded in the community. They were members of the African Methodist Episcopal (A.M.E.) Churches in Nevada City and Grass Valley. Preston Alexander was active in the major civil rights movements of the mid-19th century: abolishing slavery nationally, winning the franchise for African American men, gaining the right for African Americans to testify in court, and securing their access to public accommodations. Alexander Street is named in their honor."
decisions.reviewNotes: US Census Geocoder result accepted as a free, interpolated street-address coordinate; not storefront/building certainty.

decisions.name: Purdon Crossing Bridge
decisions.address: Bottom of Purdon Rd at South Fork of Yuba River, north of Nevada City
decisions.description: Purdon Bridge 1895. Built by Cotton Brothers of Oakland, this steel bridge replaced several wooden bridges washed away in floods. It is the only bridge of the half-through truss design remaining in California. Purdon Crossing was a vital link of the main road from Nevada City to Downieville and the Northern Mines. Note: The unique quality of a half-through truss is that the deck is carried halfway between the top and bottom of the support structure; very few were built in California. First bridge at this site was built by William E. Robinson in 1854. In 1859 John Webber dismantled Robinson's Bridge and replaced it. Webber's Bridge washed away in December 1861 and was replaced by a rope suspension foot bridge in February 1862. Jesse Wall bought the foot bridge and crossing in the spring of 1862 and built a new bridge that summer. Wall sold it to James M. Black in 1864. Peter Purdon bought the bridge in 1870 and sold it to the county in 1890, which replaced it with this one in 1895.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Railroad and Transportation Museum
decisions.address: Five Kidder Court, Nevada City. Take Ridge Rd exit off Hwy 49
decisions.description: The Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad (1876_1942) ran 22 miles from Nevada City through Grass Valley, its headquarters; to Colfax, Placer County, where it met the transcontinental line of the Central (later Southern) Pacific Railroad. Built by John F. Kidder, it was the main freight and passenger route serving the western county. Kidder ran the railroad until his death in 1901 and was followed by his widow Sarah, who became the first woman railroad president in United States. She served until 1913. This museum is dedicated to preserving the NCNGRR's place in history and to those who built, operated and maintained the little road. Second plaque dedicated 2011 by Native Sons of the Golden West: Railroad and Transportation Museum. The Nevada County Historical Society founded the Transportation Museum Division in 1983 to acquire, document, restore and preserve information and artifacts pertaining to Nevada County's railroad and transportation history. Best known is the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad that operated from 1876 until 1942. The museum opened for public enjoyment on March 17, 2003.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Red Dog Town Site
decisions.address: Red Dog Rd, 3 m. north of You Bet Rd intersection. 0.5 m. southeast of Greenhorn Creek
decisions.description: Note: The site is a federally restricted area. Red Dog (1851-1871), with a population of 2,000 at its peak, was the third town of importance in Nevada County, surviving longer than most of the earliest gold towns. The Post Office closed in 1869.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Red Ledge Stamp Mill
decisions.address: Corner of Main and Auburn Sts
decisions.description: Stamp Mill. This three stamp mill was donated to the City of Grass Valley by Mrs. Stella Tracy, owner of the famous Red Ledge Mine near Washington, Nevada County, where this mill crushed tons of gold bearing ore for many years. It is erected here in memory of the 'Cousin Jack' miners who came from Cornwall, England with their knowledge of hardrock mining used to bring gold bearing quartz to the surface from the deep mines of the area.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Reliance Hose Company No. 3
decisions.address: Race St near S. Auburn St
decisions.description: This Building, Housing Reliance Hose Company No. 3 since 1891, is hereby marked for preservation, thus honoring The Volunteer Fire Department of Grass Valley March 26, 1988 100th Anniversary of Manzanita Parlor #29, Native Daughters of the Golden West. Note: Marion Leech Conway's memoirs indicate the building was dedicated in December 1911, after housing the company for twenty years. "The doors of Reliance No. 3 have had the latch string out in welcome greeting to all when the word went out the Reliance would entertain its friends."
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Relief Hill
decisions.address: South of intersection of Relief Hill Rd and Relief Hill Mine Rd, about 5 m. east of North Bloomfield
decisions.description: Note: Access to the old townsite and historic cemetery is sometimes restricted by the US Forest Service. Description: Relief Hill dates from 1853. By 1856, it had a population of 75, two saloons, one store, one butcher shop, one blacksmith shop, two boarding houses and several homes. In 1858, registered voters alone numbered 100. Its decline followed the Sawyer decision enjoining certain aspects of hydraulic mining. In the early 1900s, some residents moved closer to Relief Hill Road. Some of those buildings can still be seen. While there is still some intermittent mining in the area, by the 1960s, the old townsite was largely deserted. Not much but the cemetery remains.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Robinson Plaza
decisions.address: Foot of Main St, Nevada City. The plaza is dedicated to Beryl Robinson and contains several sites and plaques.
decisions.description: Robinson Plaza named for Beryl Robinson Jr. Born in Nevada City September 13, 1935. City Councilman-City Manager. On July 1,1999, Beryl Robinson Jr. became the longest-serving City Manager in state history. A native of the town he has so ably served, Beryl was appointed City Manager on June 21, 1965. In addition to being Nevada City's chief administrative and financial official, he has diligently worked to preserve the community's 19th-century spirit and buildings, including the two historic structures here at Robinson Plaza. Beryl's continuing career in public service has already spanned the terms of seven presidents, six governors and three-dozen council members. A Mason, Rotarian and Elk, Beryl has brought a sense of commitment and purpose two his life matched by few and bettered by none.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Rocking Stone
decisions.address: Between High St and Keiser Ave, near Spring St
decisions.description: A natural glacial formation, or the work of an unknown tribe as a form of altar. Its exact origin will never be known. The perfectly balanced stone until recently would rock at the touch of a finger. C. F. McGlashan built the original tower in 1895 and used it to display Donner Party relics and his famous butterfly collection. Site of an overnight stop for the Olympic Torch in 1960.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Rollins Dam and Reservoir
decisions.address: Larsen Rd about 1000 ft from Orchard Springs access road to Rollins Lake, Chicago Park
decisions.description: Two plaques dedicated 1966 by Nevada Irrigation District: "Nevada Irrigation District, Yuba-Bear River Development, Rollins Dam & Reservoir Completed 1965.â€
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Root Cellar on Mexican War Land Grant
decisions.address: 16330 Gibboney Lane, Grass Valley. Take Hwy 49 to Alta Sierra intersection and follow Alta Sierra Dr to Gibboney Lane
decisions.description: This old root cellar is located on property that originally was part of a Land Grant of 160 acres granted to Josiah Dodge by President Ulysses Grant in 1873 as Bounty Land for his service as a private with Captain Steen's Co., 1st U.S. Dragoons, War with Mexico. In 1878 this property became part of the John Bree Ranch. The root cellar, built by Bree in the 1880s, was used to store his produce. Mr. Bree was among the first in the county to plant a Bartlett Pear orchard for commercial purposes.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Rough and Ready Cemetery
decisions.description: Established in 1850, it has been in continuous use, and contains approximately 400 burials. The site includes an unusual amount of well-preserved grave monuments and cast-iron fencing. The town was named by its founder, Captain A.A. Townshend, in honor of General Zachery Taylor, “Old Rough and Ready,” the 12th President of the United States.
decisions.geocodedAddress: 14472 Stagecoach Way, Rough and Ready, CA,
decisions.reviewNotes: US Census Geocoder result accepted as a free, interpolated street-address coordinate; not storefront/building certainty.

decisions.name: Rough and Ready Schoolhouse Site
decisions.address: 10079 Blue Creek Rd, south side of Old Rough and Ready Hwy, opposite Cemetery entrance
decisions.description: Note: Present owner requests privacy. Landmark: The original one-room Rough and Ready school was built in 1868. It served the community for many generations before being destroyed in the Trauner Fire of 1994. The house Kevin and Gaylene Collins built here in 1995 has an exterior like that of the schoolhouse.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Rough and Ready Toll House
decisions.description: This house and the adjacent property contain five plaques. First plaque: Built in 1859 by Benoni Thompson to collect tolls for using the Gephardt Union Turnpike running 6 miles from Penn Valley to Grass Valley. The last remaining original toll house in the West. It served as a Wells Fargo Express office and stage stop, and was the secret rendezvous place of Joaquin Murietta and Lola Montez. [Note: The reference to Joaquin Murietta and Lola Montez must be taken with a grain of salt and credited to the droll humor of E Clampus Vitus. There is no evidence of such a friendship, although anything was possible in the Old West.]
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Rough and Ready Town Site
decisions.address: Rough and Ready Hwy, corner Chapel Rd, Rough and Ready
decisions.description: Rough and Ready. Established In 1849, and named after the Rough and Ready Company of miners from Wisconsin, in honor of General Zachary Taylor, this was one of the principal towns of Nevada County. Here in 1850, articles of secession were drawn up establishing the 'Republic of Rough and Ready.' As a result of disastrous fires, only a few structures remain today of those built in the 1850s.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Rowe Saw Mill
decisions.address: 14365 Old Rough and Ready Hwy, Rough and Ready
decisions.description: Fourth plaque: Rowe Saw Mill. In the 1860s there were four saw mills in this area. This display, in honor of his founding fathers, is from the Tom Rowe Saw Mill, and displays how the mill works. The foreman stood in the cage and called out the beam size (8x8, 12x12, etc.) then the cutter set the blade which was on a track, and rode the blade down the track until the beam cut was complete. The beam then went to the mines to build the shafts.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Saint Canice Catholic Church
decisions.description: Historic St. Canice Catholic Church was built and first services held in 1864 under the guidance of Father John Griffin. Note: This also is site of St. Peter and St. Paul Catholic Church, built in 1857 and destroyed by fire in 1863.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Saint Columncille's Catholic Church
decisions.address: No. Bloomfield Rd, west of North Bloomfield, Malakoff Diggins State Park
decisions.description: The original plaque disappeared and was replaced in 1949 by Native Daughters of the Golden West by a wooden plaque: St. Columncilleâ€™s Catholic Church. Formerly the Bridgeport Union Guard Hall, was used in 1860 to train men for the Civil War under Capt. Frank Coffey of French Corral. In 1880, it was purchased as a house of worship for the Catholics of the ridge towns. Note: This building formerly was located at Birchville. In 1969 it was purchased by Babe Pinaglia and given to Malakoff Diggins Park, where it occupies the site of the Catholic Church that once served the miners of North Bloomfield.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Saint Paul Lutheran Church Site
decisions.address: St. Paul Pl. east of Hwy 174 at Chicago Park. St. Paul Cemetery is first left past the fire station
decisions.description: On this site donated by Anna Pusheck the German Evangelical Lutheran St. Paul congregation was established in 1902 for worship and praise to God, continuing until 1984. Note: Anna Pusheck was the mother of Dr. Charles Pusheck, one of the Chicago Park Colony's developers. In 1895, he built a sanitorium in Chicago Park, which he later converted to a hotel. The St. Paul Lutheran church building was dedicated in December 1904. After the church was destroyed by fire on MotherÃ
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Schallenberger Cabin Site
decisions.address: In Donner Memorial State Park, about 75 yds. east of Museum
decisions.description: Near this spot stood a small cabin built by 18-year old Moses Schallenberger and two other men. They were members of the Stephens-Townsend-Murphy party of 1844, the first pioneers to take wagons over the Sierra Nevada, opening the Truckee Route of the California Trail. The three men had volunteered to remain behind and guard six of the wagons left here by the main party. Due to extreme winter weather conditions and lack of food, the three men agreed to separate. The two older men rejoined the wagon party, but due to his weakened physical condition, Moses Schallenberger chose to remain at the cabin. The young man survived for nearly three months alone in the small cabin during the winter of 1844-45 before being rescued. His cabin would later provide shelter for the Breen family, members of the ill-fated Donner Party, who were stranded here in 1846-47. The courage and resolution of Moses Schallenberger during his solitary winter ordeal in this cabin makes him one of the true heroes in the saga of the California Trail. This monument was made possible through the generosity of many groups and individuals committed to the preservation of the California Trail. Note: The monument contains a time capsule to be opened on August 19, 2095.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Searls Law Office
decisions.description: This building completed December 18, 1872, was the law office for three generations of this family. Niles Searls, who arrived in 1851, practiced law here from 1872 to 1885. He had been elected district attorney in 1855, then district judge for Nevada, Sierra and Yuba counties, then became Chief Justice of the California Supreme Court in 1887. Fred Searls, his son, held the longest tenure in this building (1879-1929). He drafted the first incorporation papers of the hydroelectric industry of California. Carroll Searls, son of Fred, practiced here from 1915 to 1923; later became general counsel of the Newmont Mining Company in New York City; used this office until November 4,1970. Note: Niles Searls was elected district attorney in 1852, not 1855, but because he left California and went east to marry before his term of office began, William M. Stewart was appointed to fill the vacancy. Later, when Stewart resigned in 1854 to become the acting state attorney general, Searls was appointed to replace him as district attorney. From 1972 until 2014 the building housed the Historical SocietyÃ
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Shaar Zedek Grass Valley Jewish Cemetery
decisions.address: Location: Second Street off Eureka Street
decisions.description: Shaar Zedek, or the Gate of Righteousness, was established in 1856. The first burial was in 1857 and the last in 1891. Thirty headstones are visible. The cemetery was restored around 50 years ago by the Commission for the Preservation of Pioneer Jewish Cemeteries and Landmarks. It is locked and fenced to protect against vandalism.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Sheriff David Fulton Douglass
decisions.address: Just past 15934 Airport Rd, 3/4 m. from junction with Lake Vera Rd, Nevada City
decisions.description: On this spot Sheriff Douglass, a Native Son of the Golden West, gave his life July 26, 1896, bravely performing his duty. Alone he tracked a highwayman to this retreat and both fell In battle. It is believed Douglass was pitted against two and that one escaped. The bodies lay parallel. Nearby is a second plaque identifying the spot where the bandit lay.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Shoot (Chute) Hill
decisions.address: Across from space 29 in Chute Hill campground about 7/10 m. east of North Bloomfield
decisions.description: Local historians relate that water from high mountain lakes flowed thru a 500' wooden 'chute' which led to a huge square tank or pressure box behind campsite 29. Here it entered a 3' pipe reduced to gain pressure to 2', then down hill 200' to the hydraulic monitors at the mine.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Sierra Mountain Cemetery
decisions.address: East end of E. Jibboom St
decisions.description: The Masonic and Odd Fellows lodges each established cemeteries here in 1869 or 1870 to provide a burial place for their deceased. Catholics established a cemetery to the southwest. Pioneers and prominent citizens were buried within the original cemetery grounds. The less reputable were buried outside the fences. Stories abound of the bodies placed in snowbanks or ice houses awaiting the ground thaw. The Truckee Cemetery District was formed in 1946 and received title to the land and named the cemetery in 1964. In 1954 the original site increased in size despite the best efforts of the community to prevent the townfolk from dying.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Sierra Sun (Truckee Republican)
decisions.description: Oldest continuous business enterprise in the Central Sierra commenced publication in 1869 as the 'Truckee Republican' and was also informally known as the 'Truckee Screwdriver.' The definitive 'History of the Donner Party' was first published in this newspaper in 1879 when author, teacher, statesman C. F. McGlashan was its editor. Note: The Truckee Republican, which began life as the Grass Valley Republican, commenced publishing at Truckee in 1872, not 1869. Its descendant is now the Sierra Sun. The plaque was moved in 2014 from 12315 Deerfield Drive, and cannot currently be located.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Snow Tent Site
decisions.address: Near the intersection of Snow Tent and N. Bloomfield-Graniteville Rds.
decisions.description: Founded in 1850, Snow Tent flourished in the second half of the 19th century as a center for mining and lumbering and as an important stop on one of the routes through the Henness Pass. Snow Tent's best-known institution was its hotel, sometimes known as Snow Tent House. Snow Tent lies in a comparatively uninhabited part of the Tahoe National Forest, and there are no readily visible historic remains of the community.
decisions.reviewNotes: No Diana coordinate and no confident ArcGIS fallback. Address-bearing rows are eligible for the optional free Census Geocoder pass.

decisions.name: South Yuba Canal Office
decisions.description: Headquarters for the largest network of water flumes and ditches In the state. The South Yuba Canal Water Company was the first incorporated to supply water for hydraulic mining. The original ditch was in use in May, 1850, and this company office was in use from 1857 to 1880. The holdings later became the vast P.G. & E. hydro-electric system.Second plaque dedicated 1987 by Native Sons of the Golden West: US Constitution 1787 Bicentennial 1987 Nevada City, a Bicentennial Community. The plaques are mounted on adjacent brick buildings. The one on the left originally was the Potter Building and the other began life as the City Drugstore, Both survived the great fire of 1856 and are two of the oldest in Nevada City. The South Yuba Canal Water Company did not occupy the building until 1861.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: St Patrick's Cemetery
decisions.description: St. Patrickâ€™s Cemetery 1853. In memory of the pioneers buried here. A sign nearby contains this information: Site of the first Catholic Church in Grass Valley. Dedicated on October 25,1853. Rev. John Shanahan, 1st Pastor. Served as church 'till December 5, 1858. Served as the first Catholic School (1859-1879).
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Stampede Circle of Stones
decisions.address: 10600 Brockway Rd, northeast corner of Truckee River Regional Park, Truckee
decisions.description: The Stampede Circle of Stones is the oldest manmade structure in the Truckee region-at least 500 years old. Built by the Washoe or their predecessors, the Martis people, the Circle's original home was in Stampede Valley, about 10 miles northeast of here. In 1969 it was moved to this location shortly before the construction of the Stampede Dam flooded the valley. Archaeological digging on the original site indicates it may have been used as an antelope corral.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Summit City/Meadow Lake
decisions.address: Meadow Lake Rd, south of Jackson Meadows Rd
decisions.description: In June of 1863, the trapper Henry Hartley discovered gold bearing ore here and staked out the Excelsior Company claims. Exaggerated reports and a 1865 market panic in the Comstock mines of Virginia City brought thousands of miners overnight to the supposed 'Little Comstock' of Summit City. Within two years an incorporated town, renamed Meadow Lake grew to encompass 160 acres with 4,000 summer residents and over 500 buildings, including 200 business houses, a stock exchange, daily newspaper, Post Office, school, 6 breweries and 80 saloons. Streets were laid out grid fashion, with a 9,000 square foot plaza on the lakeshore. An excursion vessel ferried revelers to four hurdy-gurdy houses at the lakeâ€™s upper end. The winter of 1866-1867 brought 30 feet of snow with the 'Snowshoe Express' delivering mail through Cisco. In all the eight stamp mills in a district of 10,910 claims failed to extract the expected gold from its 'Rebellious Ore.' The optimism faded and the town declined with like rapidity. July of 1869 found 60 residents. By 1873 only Hartley 'The Hermit' remained. His grave is on a hillside to the west of town. A wooden sign nearby was erected at an unknown date by Troop 40, Kentfield-Marin Council, Boy Scouts of America: Meadow Lake-Excelsior. The granite dam was built 1858 for flumes of valley gold towns. Called Summit City with 2000 pop. after 1866. It incorporated as Meadow Lake 1866. Because gold could not be separated it was abandoned 1872.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Ten Stamp Mill
decisions.address: City parking lot on Commercial St, west of York St
decisions.description: 'Six tall, upright rods of iron, as large as a manÃ
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: The Auditorium Building
decisions.description: The Auditorium Building. On July 30, 1896, a fire started here at the site of Argallâ€™s Opera House, owned by Charles E. Clinch, burning the entire block on East Mill Street from Neal to Bank. In 1900, an incorporation composed of the Native Sons of the Golden West, Ancient Order of the Foresters, Knights of Pythias, Red Men, and the Minerâ€™s Union was formed to build a fine theater of three stories. The first floor held a magnificent theater with a capacity of 600 on the floor and 250 in the gallery and office space for the Grass Valley City Hall. The second story was devoted to lodge rooms and the third story was a banquet hall. The basement housed both the kitchen and the armory for Company 1 of the National Guard. On February 2, 1901, the Auditorium Theater opened to a week-long celebration and operated until November 12, 1922. Two days later, it reopened as the Strand Movie Theater, operating until April 22, 1942. The building was then home of the F. S. Rasco & Co. store and since 1972 has been home to Hedman/Ashley furniture store.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: The Flagg Building
decisions.description: The Flagg Bldg. The Eternal Saloon. Built in 1856 on the site of the U.S. Hotel, this brick edifice has served Nevada City as the council chamber, Schreiber's, and since 1957 Eddie Furano's Bank Club. Here at Schreiberâ€™s Felix, the marvelous, mysterious music box thundered Strauss waltzes and 'Carmen' to generations with custom and good cheer.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: The Old Post Office
decisions.description: This building Is known as the Old Post Office. It was built in 1914 by the United States Government as the Grass Valley Post Office. From 1914 until 1984 the Post Office functioned for mail, package and gold shipments from the many mines in the area. After the Post Office moved to East Main Street the building operated as an Insurance Agency in the 1980â€™s and as professional offices since October 1989. Note: Plaque was dedicated in memory of Nevada County pioneer United States Senators Aaron Augustus Sargent (1873-1879) and William Morris Stewart (1865-1875 and 1887-1905).
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: The Red Castle
decisions.description: Nevada Cityâ€™s earliest surviving brick home, built in 1860 by Judge John Williams, mine owner, businessman and civic leader, who with his family, crossed the plains in 1849. Near ruin, this house was restored by James Wade Schaar in 1963. The Red Castle stands today as a proud reminder of the part the Argonauts played in the Heritage of California.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: The Tin Can -- Dot's
decisions.description: The two modern buildings replaced structures built in the early 20th-century and used in two of Truckeeâ€™s notorious industries: bootlegging and prostitution. They upheld a tradition begun on this street in the 1870â€™s. Many a thirst was slaked and need fulfilled because of these buildings' contents. Politely ignored, although they never failed because of lack of business, they operated until mid-century to serve the working men of this and surrounding communities. Changing commercial interests and ultimately property values doomed these buildings to history.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: The Union Newspaper
decisions.address: 11464 Sutton Way, Grass Valley, in the Glenbrook Basin
decisions.description: Of the many historic newspapers started in the Northern Mines of the great Mother Lode during the famous gold rush era, only The Union of Grass Valley and Nevada City emerged a successful daily published for 100 years. Born in critical times: Civil War and presidential election. The Union has ever strived for unity and has evolved an independent voice supporting man and measure over partisanship. The Union is only the seventh daily newspaper in California to celebrate its Centennial under the original name. Centennial Date, October 28, 1964. Plaque dedicated when The Union newspaper was located on Mill Street, Grass Valley and moved with the newspaper to Sutton Way in December 1978.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: The Willo Steakhouse
decisions.description: “In 1947, Bill Davis, a retired U.S. Navy Lt. Commander, purchased and reassembled a surplus WW II Quonset hut from the Navy and opened Bill Davis's Hut, a popular watering hole for the lumber and mining workforce. In 1950, Wilma Kenny leased the property and renamed it Wilma's Hut. Since then, the business was operated by a variety of individuals. In 1963, Bob and Peggy Tucker purchased the hut and renamed it Tuck's Hut. Then in 1969 the Tuckers leased the property to Frank Williams, owner of the Willo bar in Grass Valley, who was displaced by the Gold Country Freeway construction. He partnered with Veda Folden who, with the help of her husband and brothers, converted the covered structure into the main dining room. The Willo steakhouse was born. Many folks through the decades have asked why the Willo has no 'w' on the end (Willow). The Willo is a combination of Frank Williams last name and his wife's first name Lola. In 1976 Ken and Jeannie Hiebert purchased the property from Peggy Tucker and combined the bar and restaurant operations. They operated it for 26 years before selling it to the current owners, Mike Byrne and Nancy Wilson who at the time of this dedication are committed to preserving the California roadhouse heritage of the Willo, which has served over 1,000,000 choice New York steaks."

decisions.name: Theodore Dehone Judah
decisions.address: Track side of Amtrak station, 10065 Donner Pass Rd
decisions.description: As Chief Engineer of the Central Pacific Railroad, Judah's engineering skill, political savvy and financial genius, along with the promoters' capital, culminated in the formation and construction of the Trans-Sierra leg of the first transcontinental railroad. While fighting the excesses of the railroadÃ

decisions.name: Toll Road Stake
decisions.address: 14365 Old Rough and Ready Hwy, Rough and Ready
decisions.description: Second Plaque: Toll Road Stake. Across the street from this location is a tree stump in which the original stake exists that was used to close off the passage to travelers from Grass Valley to Penn Valley. Drivers who frequented this road had to stop at this location and pay the toll. Once appropriate compensation was provided, the gate would be opened to allow passage.

decisions.name: Town of Washington
decisions.address: Bottom of Washington Rd on South Fork of the Yuba River, 18 m. northeast of Nevada City via Hwy 20
decisions.description: Washington, originally Indiana Boys Camp, is a town that refused to ghost. It was settled in 1849. By 1880 miners had recovered over $10,500,000 in gold here. Note: First plaque is on the Washington Hotel. Note: The only mining town in the county not destroyed by fire. Today, Washington (pop. about 180) is one of the few remaining early mining towns. The permanent population swells to hundreds more residents in the summer months. A second plaque, shown above, was dedicated at an unknown date in a small park at the intersection of Washington Rd and Alpha Toll Rd: In August, 1850 Washington was the highest point on the South Yuba River at which gold had been discovered, with over a 1,000 miners. In 1870 the Chinese outnumbered the white population. The last try to strike it rich was in the 1890â€™s. Washington is the last remaining village of the many that flourished in the Washington Mining District. Historical notes by Robert I. and Grace I. Slyter.

decisions.name: Trinity Episcopal Church
decisions.address: Corner of Nevada and High Sts
decisions.description: Trinity Episcopal Church. Founded as a parish in 1855, opened for Divine Service in this building on November 2, 1873. Previously on this site was â€˜Caldwell's Upper Store' built in 1849 for the miners of Upper Deer Creek Dry Diggings, now known as Nevada City, and Washington School, 1863, the communityâ€™s first high school. The present building was erected by shipwrights working in the mines. Their craftsmanship may be seen in its construction. Notes: CaldwellÃ
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Truckee Flag Pole
decisions.address: 10065 Donner Pass Rd, northwest side of Amtrak station
decisions.description: Truckee / Jewel of the Eastern Sierras. First known as Gray's Toll Station, then Coburn's Station, Truckee was established in 1863. Renowned for its lumber industry, transcontinental railroad, icehouses, saloons and red light district, Truckee has never been known for her laid back demeanor. It has always been a town where people came to entertain themselves on Friday and Saturday nights, just as it is today. Second plaque dedicated 1994 by Truckee Fire Protection District: Truckee Fire Protection District. This flagpole represents 100 years of service by the Truckee Fire Protection District and its members, past and present for their commitment to the preservation of life and property.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Truckee Jail
decisions.address: Northeast corner of Spring and Jibboom Sts
decisions.description: 1875 Old Truckee Jail. Built in September 1875 and in continuous use until 1964. Landmark registered and second plaque dedicated 1971 by Nevada County, NEV 04: The first extension of the Nevada County jail which was built in 1875 by order of the County Supervisors on the petition of residents in Meadow Lake and Truckee townships due to the high cost of transporting prisoners from the eastern part of the county to the jail in Nevada City. Built of stone at a cost of $2,500 the building housed its first prisoner in September 1875. Note: Building now houses the Truckee-Donner Historical Museum.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Truckee Round House Site
decisions.address: East end of municipal parking lot, just west of 10021 Donner Pass Rd
decisions.description: Needed to house and maintain the engines of the Central Pacific Railroad during construction of the trans-Sierra crossing, an enclosed wooden round house was constructed here in 1868. It was razed by fire in March of 1869. A 16-stall wooden structure was built that year as the maintenance facility of the C.P.R.R., Truckee Division. Another fire in 1876 resulted in construction of a 22-stall granite and wooden structure. This served through 1942, when it was officially retired, a victim of the end of the steam era and consolidation of maintenance at other locations. The round houses were home to helper engines, fire trains and the first rotary snow plow in the Sierra.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Truckee's First Log Cabin
decisions.description: First Log Cabin. Erected by Joseph Gray at S.W. corner of present day Bridge and Spring streets to serve Dutch Flat Wagon Road traffic over Donner Pass. Removed to this site 1907.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Truckee's Vigilance ("601") Committee
decisions.address: On sidewalk in front of 10096 Donner Pass Rd, across street from Amtrak Station,
decisions.description: Truckeeâ€™s vigilance committee, known as the 601, was born in response to the rise in crime and 'undesirables' which plagued the young railroad town. In late 1874 and early 1875, the 601 carried out a campaign to intimidate and rid the town of those elements. After accidentally shooting one of their own members, the 601 disbanded until August 1889 when they reassembled for one last time. Though their goals were never fully achieved, the 601 did bring a semblance of law and order to the area through their vigilance.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Union Hill
decisions.address: 10879 Bartlett Dr, just off Hwy 174, Grass Valley CA
decisions.description: Union Hill School was established in March 1868 as a one-room schoolhouse on the grounds of Union Hill Mine. On June 26, 1875, an argument over a trustee election took place at the schoolhouse, resulting in a stabbing and subsequent death. Three days later an arsonist burned down the schoolhouse. In the late 1870s, a new schoolhouse was built on the current location and was in service until 1940. The modern building has been in continuous use since 1940. Union Hill has proudly served our community for over 144 years.

This memorial flagstaff dedicated in eternal memory of those war veterans resting within the portals of this cemetery who served in the wars of our nation and those who gave their lives defending AmericaÃ

decisions.name: Victory Highway Monument
decisions.address: Northwest corner of Spring St and Donner Pass Rd, Truckee
decisions.description: First plaque dedicated 1928. Text of plaque: Dedicated to Californiaâ€™s sons and daughters who served their country in the World War 1917-1918 and to the memory of those who gave the last full measure of devotion. Second plaque and monument dedicated 1998 by Truckee Town Council and California Department of Transportation: Victory Highway Monument Rededication July 24, 1998. This monument was originally sited on old Highway 40 near the California/Nevada line in 1928 and removed in the mid 1970s due to vandalism. It was one of a series of monuments placed on the 'Victory Highway' across the U.S., honoring those who died serving the United States in World War I. Donner Pass Road and part of Glenshire Drive through Truckee still follow the basic alignment of the old highway. The Town of Truckee, in cooperation with CalTrans, has rededicated this monument not only for its original purpose and the history of Old Highway 40, but also for current revitalization efforts occurring in historic downtown Truckee.

decisions.description: Plaques dedicated 1966 and 1974 by Hague-Thomas-Hegarty (now Frank Gallino) Post No. 130, American Legion: 1966 plaque: Gary Ames Miller Memorial. This memorial is dedicated to all American servicemen and women of the Vietnam War in the name of Gary Ames Miller, United States Marine Corps, the first local serviceman to give the full measure for God and country in this conflict. All Americans owe an endless debt of gratitude to the gallant men and women of our armed services for preserving our way of life, a life endowed with justice, freedom and democracy. 1974 plaque: Western Nevada County servicemen killed in action in Vietnam: Edward Alien Ownry 09/09/65, Gary Ames Miller 06/01/66, Douglas A. decisions.name: W.C Jones Memorial Hospital
decisions.description: Built in 1867 for the Faheys, owners of the Allison Ranch mine, then owned by William Campbell, a prominent local businessman, the building was acquired in 1905 by Dr. John Taylor Jones for use as the county's first private hospital. He named it the Dr. W. C. Jones Memorial Hospital for his father, who been the most prominent physician and surgeon in the county in the 19th century. After John's death in 1917, his younger brother, Dr. Carl, continued the practice. The hospital opened in 1907 and served patients until 1968, being the area's principal medical center for most of that time. In 1975, it was acquired by Howard Levine and his wife the late Peggy Swan. Renamed the Swan Levine House, it is used as an arts academy and bed and breakfast inn.

decisions.name: Washington Brewery
decisions.description: Founded 1858 by John Frank, the brewery operated until prohibition. The ownership remains in the Frank family to the present time. Note: The Frank family sold the building in 1973. It no longer functions as a brewery.

decisions.name: Washington Elementary School
decisions.description: 100 Years of uninterrupted use as a California public school. Having survived fires, floods, wars, time, and politics, this school has been doing its part to ensure the rights of American children to receive a basic education.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Washington Hotel and Site
decisions.description: Hessel Buisman built the first hotel on this site in 1857. It was destroyed by fire in August 1867, along with neighboring businesses. Buisman replaced it with a two-story hotel in that same year. In 1887, Buisman's daughter Harminia and her husband, E. T. Worthley, inherited the hotel, which burned in April 1896. The Worthleys reopened it with a third story in August 1896. John J. Rogers bought the hotel in 1910 for $50, and in 1911 he installed a water pipeline across the river to the Nice and Latta ditch to furnish water for the town. In 1912, he used the water to generate the first electricity for the hotel. Thomas B. Williamson, who had purchased a half interest in the hotel for $10 in 1911, bought out Rogers in 1916, and then sold it to Pietro Orzalli. Orzalli sold the property to R. F. Williamson (brother of Tom) in 1925. In 1926, Emory J. Haverstock bought the hotel and added dormer windows on the top floor, using lumber salvaged from the fire-ravaged Dugan Hotel (also in Washington). Haverstock sold it to Clark and Edna Waite for $10 in 1939. In 1955, the hotel was sold for $10 to Harold Moneyhun, former owner of the Holbrooke Hotel in Grass Valley, and Edwin Furano of Nevada City. In 1968, Thomas E. Walsh used siding salvaged from the National Hotel Annex in Nevada City to enclose the side porch on the upriver side of the hotel.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Washoe Tribe Grinding Stone-Lam
decisions.address: Northeast corner of Jibboom and Bridge Sts
decisions.description: Plaque placed 2017: Washoe Tribe Grinding Stone-Lam. The Washoe Tribe are the original inhabitants of the Truckee-Tahoe region. The Washoe people utilized bed rock mortar for food processing, they call it a Lam. Much of the Washoe heritage resources have been lost due to development. This Lam is a casualty of development, displaced from its original location and is an example of past practices. Working together, the Town of Truckee, Mountain Area Preservation and the Washoe Tribe agreed to place the Lam at the Trout Creek Pocket Park in order to keep it close to its original location and as a reminder of the original people who lived in the region.

decisions.name: Weiss-Hieronimus Brewery Cellar
decisions.description: The Weiss-Hieronimus Brewery Cellar. Beginning in the early 1860s and continuing until Prohibition, the lower portion of this building was the storeroom for keg beer produced first by Emille (sic) Weiss for the Weiss Brewery then by Simon Hieronimus for the Hieronimus Brewery_later known as the Nevada Brewery. As the brewery business grew, Mr. Hieronimus established a bottling plant and stable on property across the street from this building. But keg beer continued to be stored here. A fire in the summer of 1896 destroyed the original house and severely damaged the cellar. But the Nevada Brewery emerged bigger and better than ever. It was at that time that the cellar was enlarged to its present size, a ventilation system installed and refrigeration added. In 2004 the property was acquired by Jesse and Kay Connor, who carefully restored the old brewery cellar and home. This plaque has been placed to honor the legacy of both the Weiss and Hieronimus families and to remind people that the best lager beer in Nevada County was once produced here. Note: Messrs Weiss and Hieronimus were each born in France. Weiss arrived in Nevada City in 1850 and immediately opened a brewery. Hieronimus arrived in Nevada City in 1876, then operated a brewery in North Bloomfield before taking over this brewery.

decisions.name: Western America Skisport Museum
decisions.address: Exit on Boreal Ridge Rd on I-80 west of summit
decisions.description: A monument and statue was placed by Snowshoe Thompson Lodge No. 78, Sons of Norway, Yuba City: Snowshoe Thompson. A hero of the Sierra Nevada mountains! Probably the first skier of the West. This Viking Son of Norway exemplified the spirit of a true pioneer: Strong, daring, faithful and courageous. He was the answer to CaliforniaÃ

decisions.name: Wolf Post Office
decisions.description: The Nevada County Historical Landmarks Commission is pleased to announce the installation and dedication of a plaque honoring the Wolf Post Office on Sunday, October 6, 2019. Ranchers and farmers began to settle the historic agricultural community of Wolf in the 1860s. William Sweet arrived in Wolf in 1869, immigrating from Cornwall, and purchased 200 acres before his wife and children joined him in 1871. On August 24, 1888, a post office was established for the growing population of Wolf. John Sweet, one of Williamâ€™s sons, ran the post office from the living room of his ranch house and it soon became a community center and gathering place for approximately 75 residents of Wolf. It is believed that John Sweet was the longest serving U.S. Postmaster, continually running the Wolf Post Office for 52 years until his nephew W. B. Sweet took over on February 19, 1940. W. B. moved the Post Office from his uncleâ€™s home to a small building close by and served the Wolf community until 1956 when the post office was decommissioned with only 2 residents utilizing the service.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Woman's Christian Temperance Union
decisions.address: North side of Neal St between Mill and S. Church Sts, Grass Valley
decisions.description: The first Woman's Christian Temperance Union in California organized in Grass Valley March 25, 1874. Rededicated and second plaque placed 2014: Originally placed near the corner of Neal and South Church Streets near the site of the old Congregational Church and the Women's Improvement Club building. Note: In 1972, the Congregational Church building was demolished to make way for a bank building. The first plaque was removed and stored in the nearby Methodist Church. In 2014, it was rededicated by the Manzanita Parlor of the Native Daughters at its present location on a monument constructed from petrified wood.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: Woolman School
decisions.description: Woolman School was the first Quaker boarding school in the western United States. The school was named for John Woolman, a prominent 18th century Quaker known for, among other things, his opposition to slavery, and his efforts to promote peace among settlers and indigenous tribes. Though no longer a boarding school, the campus still provides educational programs.
decisions.reviewNotes: No Diana coordinate and no confident ArcGIS fallback. Address-bearing rows are eligible for the optional free Census Geocoder pass.

decisions.name: World War I Memorial, Grass Valley
decisions.address: Memorial Park, corner of Race St and Colfax Ave, Grass Valley
decisions.description: Plaque dedicated 1921: Honor Roll. Heroes from the Grass Valley District who gave their lives for their country 1917-1918: Anthony J. Bevilacqua, Robert H. Beyer, Lawrence A. Brasher, Melvyn Frandy, Isaac Leslie Fleming, Hedley Hill, Lenville Dooley Jatunn, William Hague, William A. Kneebone, Albert Pinkham, William Hawley Moran, James Rodgers, Clarence Crase Thomas, J. M. Spencer, Elton Thomas Whiting, John Edward White. Recently, a small plaque at the base of the main plaque added U.S. Army Nurse Elina W. Hill to the Honor Roll. Note: The above inscription is on a very large monument. Surrounding it is a grove of 16 trees, each with a marker in front inscribed with the name of one of these men and the American Legion logotype.
decisions.reviewNotes: Diana Workbook supplied valid Nevada County coordinates.

decisions.name: World War II & Korean War Memorial
decisions.address: Memorial Park, corner of Race St and Colfax Ave, Grass Valley
decisions.description: Plaque dedicated by Grass Valley Elks No. 538 and Frank Gallino Post No. 130 American Legion: World War II 1941-1946 Korean 1950-1955. To the men and women who made the supreme sacrifice for our country and her noble purpose, in memory of those who died that liberty might live. Note: Plaque is on a concrete monument that includes a flagpole and benches.

decisions.name: You Bet Town Site
decisions.address: Red Dog Rd, between Lowell Hill and Chalk Bluff Rds, six m. east of You Bet Rd exit on Hwy 174
decisions.description: This mining camp, established between 1858-1860, consisted of 40 or 50 buildings by 1864. A school house was built midway between You Bet and Red Dog. The town was destroyed by fire in April 1869, partially rebuilt, and burned again in September 1873 and August 1915. The courts halted most hydraulic mining in 1884, but gold mining continued until the 1940s. The You Bet Post Office was closed in 1903.

decisions.name: CA Historical Marker- Floriston (Bronco)
decisions.description: Established in 1872 as Bronco, this railroad wood stop became a noted ice and lumber center and was renamed in 1891. The Floriston Pulp and Paper Company built the present town after 1899 and operated one of the world's largest pulp mill through 1930. It paper products and ice helped California's agricultural industry feed the hungry of the world.
decisions.reviewNotes: US Census Geocoder result accepted as a free, interpolated street-address coordinate; not storefront/building certainty.

decisions.name: Uncle Joe's Cabin
decisions.description: Joseph Gray believed that this area would be a good place for a tavern and way station for weary travelers. It was near this spot that transportation routes converged—the Henness Pass route from the upper North Fork of the Yuba, and the Dutch Flat-Donner Lake turnpike. Five years hence the Central Pacific would choose the same area as the focal point for its mountain operations. Constructed of native tamarack and lodgepole pine in about 1858, Gray’s cabin was located at today’s southwest corner of Jibboom and Bridge Streets. Experts have examined the 24’x20’ building and noticed no evidence of any kind of saw used to build the structure. The timbers had all been hewn with axes into the shape of a house.
decisions.reviewNotes: No Diana coordinate and no confident ArcGIS fallback. Address-bearing rows are eligible for the optional free Census Geocoder pass.

decisions.name: The Truckee Hotel
decisions.description: Established in 1873, the Truckee Hotel is a staple in tales of Truckee's past. First built as the American House, later known as the Whitney House, the structure stands tall on the historical registry. Touched by many as a stagecoach stop on the Dutch Flat Wagon Road, the Truckee Hotel remains a drop-in for Sierra visitors, historians, adventure seekers, foodies, actors, and musicians. In 1976 the hotel was renamed The Truckee Hotel, and in 1992, extensive exterior renovations restored the building to its present day appearance. The recent interior remodel with warm earth tone color carpeting, linens, finishings, amenities and rich wood complements the fresh mountain air as you ease into your stay with us.

decisions.description: Our story began in 1983 when Jerry Cirino first opened the doors to Cirino’s Bar & Grill, an Italian Restaurant in Nevada City, Ca offering “time honored” Italian recipes from the Cirino family kitchen. This old-world tradition of using only the freshest seasonal ingredients, along with the Cirino standard of quality and flavor, left our friends and guests wanting more. Cirino’s at Main Street boasts a menu of 70 items as well as an offering of daily specials. decisions.category: Artisan Place to Eat, Drink or Stay
decisions.description: Before there was a hotel, there was a bar. In 1852, thousands of thirsty prospectors filled their glasses at the original Golden Gate Saloon. Our favorite watering hole has always been tenacious and inventive: after Grass Valley was destroyed by a fire in 1855, the original owners quickly adapted and sold whiskey and beer to loyal customers from a tent among the rubble the next day.

decisions.name: Sierra Starr Vineyard & Winery (Store)
decisions.category: Artisan Place to Eat, Drink or Stay
decisions.description: The Starr family has been producing award winning wines in the Sierra Foothills since 1996. We take pride in crafting small lot wines from our estate vineyards, specializing in Zinfandel, Sauvignon Blanc and Cabernet Franc (along with many others). We strive for our wines to express our unique terroir and showcase varietal character at its finest. Utilizing our under ground-gravity flow winery, solar power and dry farming, we sustainably craft our wines with passion. We welcome you to taste these wines and experience all that downtown Grass Valley, the heart of the gold country, has to offer.

decisions.name: The Holbrooke Hotel
decisions.category: Artisan Place to Eat, Drink or Stay
decisions.description: Celebrating Grass Valley at its most authentic…unearthed, restored and brought forward into today. Welcome to the Holbrooke Hotel: where modern luxuries blend seamlessly with a historically unique, rustic-yet-refined, one-of-a-kind property. Set in the heart of our bustling town with easy access to restaurants, tasting rooms, shops and historical sights, our meticulously renovated California Historical Landmark offers a true break from the ordinary and an unforgettable stay. So dust off your boots and sit a spell! After serving the community for over 150 years, the Holbrooke Hotel is ready to spend some time with you.

decisions.description: Thirsty Barrel opened it’s doors officially in Dec. 1, 2017. After months of searching for a great place to open a business and a great place to live, it became clear that the Grass Valley and Nevada County area is the right choice. We look forward to providing you the best service and the greatest possible products we can. We offer 30 taps of constantly rotating craft beers, great local wines, and great food to compliment both. Plus we love dogs, bring your furry friend along to our dog freindly patio. As we grow, it is our hopes that we become embedded as a staple in Grass Valley and Nevada County because we love our community.

decisions.name: BrewBilt Brewing Company
decisions.category: Artisan Place to Eat, Drink or Stay
decisions.description: BrewBilt Brewing was founded in 2021 with a deliberate focus on balanced and approachable beers made with locally sourced craft malt. This commitment to Sacramento Valley-grown barley, wheat, and oats supports local craft-minded producers like Admiral Maltings, reduces the carbon footprint of our production, and makes damn tasty beer that maintains freshness better than beer made from commodity malts. In December 2023, we opened our taproom and restaurant BrewBilt BrewHaus in downtown Nevada City. BrewHaus shares the brewery's devotion to European craft quality and local ingredients, served up by friendly faces in a relaxed atmosphere. We also have the sunniest patio in town!

decisions.name: Mountain Ranch Winery
decisions.category: Artisan Place to Eat, Drink or Stay
decisions.description: The secret that makes Mountain Ranch Winery special stems from winemaker Bob Hoffman’s passion for winemaking. Inside every bottle is a combination of science, history, geology, psychology and a little bit of magic. Although Mountain Ranch Winery was established in 2003, Bob’s interest goes back 35 years to when he realized that sharing a good bottle of wine transcends a meal, bonds a friendship, and transforms an event into a celebration. At Mountain Ranch Winery, wine tasting is an experience that goes beyond the palette. At each tasting, Bob will share his vast knowledge of wines as well as interesting stories of his travels in the wine business. While wines from Mountain Ranch Winery are available in fine restaurants across the country, Bob is able to keep the prices affordable by selling directly from the winery. In 2020, Bob and his future wife Lisa were fortunate to find a beautiful 6.5-acre property in Grass Valley with a winery already on the property, and a large garage with space for a pottery studio. Lisa takes a ball of clay and transforms it into a one of a kind piece of pottery. Her functional pieces of pottery are on display and for sale at the winery. Comfortable. That is how we want our customers to feel while at the winery. So we set it up to feel like you are welcomed into our home. It’s a place to relax, try delicious wines, shop for pottery, play a round of Bocce Ball, and make new friends. Wine and art, laughter and fun. That is what Mountain Ranch Winery is about.

decisions.name: Alta Sierra Wine Shop
decisions.category: Artisan Place to Eat, Drink or Stay
decisions.description: The Alta Sierra Wine Shop was opened in 2009 by Tina and Ray Yedding with Dave Luce as a silent partner. Dave bought the shop from them in 2011. His mission as the owner of the shop was to search out beautiful wines featuring the small, the artisanal, the producers of wine with integrity who respected their workers and the land. Dave continued his commitment to this pursuit for the 15 years he owned and ran the wine shop. In addition to offering beautiful wines at the best price point, he created a unique space that is welcoming, friendly, safe, and fun. Many friendships have been forged not only between Dave and his customers, but between the customers themselves at the Alta Sierra Wine Shop. This, more than anything, makes the Alta Sierra Wine Shop special. My name is Dana Price. Dave sold the shop to me July 1 of 2024. It is my utmost pleasure to be the owner of this precious shop. I will continue the legacy of offering beautiful wines at the best price, offer the community opportunities to fund raise with wine tasting events, and facilitate the unique, caring, friendly, safe, and inclusive culture that is the Alta Sierra Wine Shop.

Backed with culinary and agricultural backgrounds, we pride ourselves by providing humanely raised meat and seasonally sourced produce to our wonderful community! Join us for a dining experience like no other at The "Historic” Owl Tavern (originally established in 1852), where culinary excellence meets a cozy, enchanting atmosphere. Savor innovative dishes inspired by local flavors, prepared with the finest seasonal produce. Enjoy our expertly crafted cocktails, featuring unique blends and house-made infusions. Relax in our warm, inviting space designed to make every meal a memorable occasion. Our friendly staff is dedicated to providing top-notch service and ensuring your visit is nothing short of perfect. We look forward to providing a level of culinary expertise and service that is worth remembering. Whether it’s a special celebration, a casual meal, or a night out with friends, we promise to make it magical.

decisions.decisionId: avanguardia-winery-nevada-city--artisan places to eat drink or--row-60
decisions.name: Avanguardia Winery
decisions.category: Artisan Place to Eat, Drink or Stay
decisions.description: A small (actually ultra-boutique) winery, we are the home of non-traditional blended wines, with the majority of our wines being proprietary blends crafted from 2-6 different grape varieties, many of which are Italian, and all of which are pretty much out of the mainstream. Check out our page on Our Varieties. Avanguardia does not much use the more common varietals, like Cabernet, Zinfandel, or Chardonnay. Our flagship white wine, CRISTALLO, features a grape from the former Soviet Republic of Georgia in Eastern Europe. Our most unique red, AMPIO, combines the UC-patented cross, Carmine, with the French Mondeuse for a taste sensation that is uniquely different. Our newest blend, TRE "T", after the three international varieties used (Teroldego, Tempranillo, and Tannat) is a wine of deep color and great substance, already recognized with Gold at the San Francisco Chronicle competition. Our emphasis is always on balance, with just the right amount of alcohol, acidity, tannin or oak, avoiding contemporary trends towards excess in these areas; in some ways our wines are more like Europe than California. Dare to be different and try us out---you won't be disappointed! You can visit us at the Winery, five miles due west of Nevada City, or at the Tasting Room at 163 Mill Street (pictured when you view our wine portfolio).
